elements.sent-2411-029: Among the most well-studied classes of quantum error-correcting codes, stabilizer codes are perhaps the most general.
elements.sent-2411-030: The conventional description of a stabilizer code is given by a stabilizer tableau, a $(n-k) \times n$ grid consisting of $n-k$ Pauli strings $\set{I, X, Y, Z ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The code is then defined to be the simultaneous $+1$ eigenspace of the $n-k$ strings.
elements.sent-2411-032: Throughout the history of quantum error correction (QEC), the stabilizer tableau has proven incredibly useful as a description of most of the codes we study.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, there is a significant distinction between a good description and a good constructive tool.
elements.sent-2411-034: Simply by staring at an array of Pauli strings, it is not at all obvious as to which arrays are good codes.
elements.sent-2411-035: For this reason, most constructive techniques have relied on more specific techniques that generate stabilizer codes from classical codes, which are far more well-understood.
elements.sent-2411-036: Such techniques include the CSS product as well as modern variants like the hypergraph product code~\cite{calderbank1996good,tillich2013quantum,breuckmann2021balanced , as well as topological constructions of surface codes~\cite{kitaev2003fault .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: A second shortcoming of the stabilizer tableau description is that it is often difficult to perform analysis of algorithms for quantum codes on it directly.
elements.sent-2411-038: Decoding algorithms, for example, are constructed in surface-type codes by utilizing matching algorithms on graphs.
elements.sent-2411-039: One commonality in these two approaches is the prevalent appearance of graphs in each.
elements.sent-2411-040: CSS codes are built from classical codes, and in turn classical codes have benefited heavily from graph representations.
elements.sent-2411-041: In particular, by constructing Tanner graphs from the parity check matrix of a classical code, one can use interesting graph properties such as spectral expansion to build better codes.
elements.sent-2411-042: These realizations led to significant advancements in classical coding theory~\cite{tanner1981recursive,sipser1996expander .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Likewise, surface-type codes are essentially lattice representations of certain families of stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this sense such codes also provide evidence that good codes can be constructed by analyzing objects with naturally pre-existing topological and geometrical structure.
elements.sent-2411-045: With such a rich history of the contributions of graphs in coding theory, we are motivated to seek the most general way in which graphs can represent stabilizer codes in hopes that such a representation will not only yield an elegant description of stabilizer codes, but also provide guidance based on insights in geometry, topology, and spectral graph theory for two tasks.
elements.sent-2411-046: The first is the flexible construction of good codes for a particular experimental task.
elements.sent-2411-047: That is, given a desired rate, distance, geometric constraints, and stabilizer weight constraints, can we reason by graphs to generate a code which meets as many of these desires as possible? The second is the improved construction and analysis of coding algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-048: We consider especially the tasks of decoding, calculating the distance, and reducing stabilizer weight.
elements.sent-2411-049: In the stabilizer tableau representation, each of these tasks are not obvious even for relatively simple-looking tableaus, e.g. the 5-qubit code.
elements.sent-2411-050: By contrast, decoding and distance calculation are both performed in surface codes by exploiting graph-type properties, and many Tanner graph-based classical codes which build CSS codes operate in a similar fashion.
elements.sent-2411-051: There is reason to believe, therefore, that if we can find a general graphical representation of stabilizer codes it will enable us to extend at least some of these positive results to other families of codes (but not all, due to the general computational hardness of, e.g., distance calculation~\cite{kapshikar2023hardness ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our first contribution in this paper is the development of precisely such a general graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-053: Specifically, we devise a family of graphs with $n+k$ nodes (intended to represent a $\llbracket n, k\rrbracket$ code) which take a semi-bipartite form.
elements.sent-2411-054: The $k$ inputs may not be connected to each other but are allowed to connect to the $n$ output nodes, and the $n$ output nodes can connect to each other.
elements.sent-2411-055: We further impose some rules to ensure that the graph representation is unique, i.e. two distinct stabilizer codes map to distinct graphs.
elements.sent-2411-056: Intuitively speaking, the input-output edges visualize the propagation of information from logical to physical qubits, while the output structure visualizes the entanglement structure of the physical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-057: We give an efficient algorithm mapping any stabilizer tableau to its graph form, and further prove that this map is essentially bijective.
elements.sent-2411-058: In other words, given a graph, it is also possible to efficiently construct the corresponding stabilizer tableau.
elements.sent-2411-059: The inverse map is much more interesting in many senses---it enables us to build stabilizer codes directly by constructing sufficiently good graphs, for some reasonable definition of ``good" which we explore in this work.
elements.sent-2411-060: The graph to stabilizer code correspondence, however, extends much further than construction of codes alone.
elements.sent-2411-061: We are able to define a simple one-player game, which we refer to as quantum lights out (QLO), that can encode a wide range of coding tasks at once.
elements.sent-2411-062: A QLO game occurs on a graph, with each node having a switch and a light.
elements.sent-2411-063: Roughly speaking, a QLO game begins with some starting configuration of lights being on or off, and the goal is to turn all lights off in as few moves as possible.
elements.sent-2411-064: Each move consists of choosing a node and either (a) flipping the switch, which toggles all lights of the node's neighbouring lights and permanently turns off the node's own light or (b) only permanently turning off the node's own light.
elements.sent-2411-065: Because optimal strategies of a simple game on a graph are more easily analyzed, we are optimistic that analysis of QLO games may lead to a generically improved understanding of quantum coding algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-066: To provide evidence for such claims, we demonstrate the advantage of a graph representation in three ways.
elements.sent-2411-067: The first is constructive, by designing specific codes easily with graphs.
elements.sent-2411-068: The second is random code analysis.
elements.sent-2411-069: The third is the design of a simple and efficient graph decoding algorithm on a family of codes by adoption of a greedy strategy in a QLO game.
elements.sent-2411-070: When we compile the 9-qubit Shor code~\cite{shor1995scheme , 7-qubit Steane code~\cite{steane1996multiple , and 5-qubit code~\cite{gottesman2009introduction into their graphs, we find that they respectively take the form of a star, a cube, and a pentagon (see Figure~\ref{fig:smallcodesdodeca ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: These remarkably simple structures not only immediately suggest generalizations of each code into infinite families, i.e. recursively defined stars, hypercubes and polyhedra, and $n$-gons, but also imply that other graphs with similarly nice geometrical properties have the potential to be good codes.
elements.sent-2411-072: To explore this idea, we construct numerous small codes by examining highly regular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-073: We present a novel non-CSS code based on the graph of a dodecahedron with parameters $\llbracket16,4,3\rrbracket$, outperforming the rate of the best non-CSS code previously known to us, the 5-qubit code, both seen in Figure~\ref{fig:smallcodesdodeca .}
elements.sent-2411-074: While the graph of an icosahedron does not make for a good code, we do construct a code based on a five-fold covering of an icosahedron, with parameters $\llbracket54,6,5\rrbracket$.
elements.sent-2411-075: These examples show the ability of highly structured graphs to produce interesting codes and show that larger covering spaces of graphs can also help with code search, with recursively defined stars being reminiscent of Cayley graphs of free groups.
elements.sent-2411-076: We also provide suggestions for constructing codes based on certain experimental needs.
elements.sent-2411-077: For example, if we desire a high-distance code that can be embedded into two-dimensional space, we can use a high-order hyperbolic tiling to fit more edges around each vertex into a plane.
elements.sent-2411-078: Alternatively, if the planar condition is not required and we are interested in graphs of higher degree, we can construct families of high-dimensional grid lattice graphs as they are easy to work with and have a degree that increases with the dimension.
elements.sent-2411-079: These graphs can also optionally be placed on a torus for periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-080: These do not necessarily need to be CSS codes, as this depends on the parity of the torus dimensions, and so would differ from existing families of CSS codes, such as LDPC codes or hypergraph product codes.
elements.sent-2411-081: These examples showcase the versatility in constructing codes with desirable properties, achieved by simply choosing an appropriate graph.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our graph representations can be used to build codes both large and small, while being extremely customizable and simple to work with.
elements.sent-2411-083: Next, we study random coding in the context of the graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-084: Under a stabilizer tableau description, one can show via a probabilistic method that, asymptotically, there exist codes with high distance and rate, subject to a rate-distance tradeoff.
elements.sent-2411-085: This result is known as the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov (qGV) bound.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, the qGV probabilistic method is very coarse because it picks random Pauli strings to be stabilizers, subject to the condition that they are independent and commuting.
elements.sent-2411-087: As a consequence, the stabilizer weight tends to be very close to maximal.
elements.sent-2411-088: Randomness on graphs can be studied in a much more fine-grained manner, such as by selectively deciding which nodes can be connected at all and with what probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-089: Each choice yields a distribution over the corresponding stabilizer code space.
elements.sent-2411-090: By leveraging this fine-grained randomness, we construct a distribution of stabilizer codes such that with very high probability, a random code in this class will have a desired rate and distance, as well as a bounded stabilizer weight that depends on rate and distance.
elements.sent-2411-091: Importantly, this distribution is not the uniform distribution over stabilizer codes as in the case of the qGV bound, but rather a subset of sufficiently local codes.
elements.sent-2411-092: The graph structure enables us to easily control this locality in a way that is not obvious in the absence of a graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-093: As a result, we obtain a more flexible three-way tradeoff between distance, rate, and stabilizer weight, extending the versatility beyond the qGV bound.
elements.sent-2411-094: Thirdly, we examine decoding algorithms constructed by studying the graphs of codes.
elements.sent-2411-095: We compute both the stabilizers and the logical operators of codes from their graphs in the general case.
elements.sent-2411-096: We show that these inherit locality properties from the graph.
elements.sent-2411-097: Additionally, we demonstrate a bijection between certain vertices and stabilizers, allowing us to represent a syndrome as a set of lights on vertices that must be turned off.
elements.sent-2411-098: We work out exactly how various errors on the graph manipulate this syndrome and explicitly construct a QLO game.
elements.sent-2411-099: We derive that turning off all the lights is equivalent to decoding a set of errors and that the number of moves taken is the weight of the recovery operation.
elements.sent-2411-100: Thus, an algorithm that plays QLO optimally will always decode a received, possibly error-filled message to the closest codeword.
elements.sent-2411-101: We demonstrate our results by constructing several families of graphs based on high-dimensional grid lattices, including a family of boolean hypercube graphs, which include a code with parameters $\llbracket112,16,7\rrbracket$.
elements.sent-2411-102: We present an efficient algorithm for playing QLO from small initial configurations on these graphs.
elements.sent-2411-103: In particular, we prove that our algorithm can always correct any small enough set of errors.
elements.sent-2411-104: Here, small enough means at most half of the largest correctable number of errors.
elements.sent-2411-105: Additionally, we give evidence for the algorithm's optimality, its ability to correct up to the largest correctable number of errors, which is half of the distance of the code.
elements.sent-2411-106: These findings show promise for search for codes by constructing graphs on which there exist efficient algorithms for playing QLO in the presence of few enough errors.
elements.sent-2411-107: Our findings in this work show both that the graph representation holds promise for the construction/analysis of quantum codes and that there is significant room for the exploration of applications of graph theory results to codes via this representation.
elements.sent-2411-108: In future work we plan to deeply explore how certain properties of graphs, e.g. spectral expansion, geometry, and connectivity to name a few, correspond to properties of codes.
elements.sent-2411-109: We also plan to study strategies on QLO games in simple initial configurations, so as to devise techniques that apply at once to decoding, weight reduction, and distance approximation.
elements.sent-2411-110: Individual codes we have found so far (all are 3-away) (note that 3-away codes have max stab weight $2d$ (though in practice it's a little smaller for many of these):.
elements.sent-2411-111: Code family: $d$-dimensional square grid on torus (2-away, check weight complicated).
elements.sent-2411-112: While stabilizer tableaus have proven exceptionally useful as a descriptive tool for additive quantum codes, they otherwise offer little guidance for concrete constructions or coding algorithm analysis.
elements.sent-2411-113: We introduce a representation of $\llbracket n, k\rrbracket$ stabilizer codes as graphs with certain structures.
elements.sent-2411-114: Specifically, the graphs take a semi-bipartite form wherein $k$ ``input'' nodes map to $n$ ``output'' nodes, such that output nodes may connect to each other but input nodes may not.
elements.sent-2411-115: Intuitively, the graph's input-output edges represent information propagation of the encoding circuit, while output-output edges represent the code's entanglement structure.
elements.sent-2411-116: We prove that this graph representation is in bijection with tableaus and give an efficient compilation algorithm that transforms tableaus into graphs.
elements.sent-2411-117: We then show that this map is efficiently invertible, which gives a new universal recipe for code construction by way of finding graphs with sufficiently nice properties.
elements.sent-2411-118: The graph representation gives insight into both code construction and algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-119: To the former, we argue that graphs provide a flexible platform for building codes particularly at smaller (non-asymptotic) scales.
elements.sent-2411-120: We construct as examples constant-size codes, e.g. a $[[64, 6, 5]]$ code and a family of $[[n, \Theta(\frac{n {\log n ), \Th(\log n)]]$ codes.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: We also leverage graphs in a probabilistic analysis to extend the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov bound into a three-way distance-rate-weight tradeoff.
elements.sent-2411-122: To the latter, we show that key coding algorithms---distance approximation, weight reduction, and decoding---are unified as instances of a single optimization game on a graph.
elements.sent-2411-123: Moreover, key code properties such as distance, weight, and encoding circuit depth, are all controlled by the graph degree.
elements.sent-2411-124: We give efficient algorithms for producing simple encoding circuits whose depths scale as twice the degree and for implementing logical diagonal and certain Clifford gates with non-constant but reduced depth.
elements.sent-2411-125: Finally, we construct a simple efficient decoding algorithm and prove a performance guarantee for a certain class of graphs, including the $[[n, \Theta(\frac{n {\log n ), \Th(\log n)]]$ code.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: These results give evidence that graphs are generically useful for the study of stabilizer codes and their practical implementations.
elements.sent-2411-127: Introduction Most quantum algorithms which are believed to provide significant speedups to certain computational problems require both many qubits and error correction to succeed in practice~\cite{t1,t2,t3,t4,t5,t6,t7,nielsen2002quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-128: As advancements in the experimental scaling of quantum computers have steadily marched forward, the problem of designing good quantum codes for the practical implementation of various quantum algorithm has in turn become of more pressing interest~\cite{e1,e2,e3,e4,e5 .}
elements.sent-2411-129: With a few exceptions, stabilizer codes have emerged as one of the most intensely studied families of codes due to their simple description~\cite{ec1,ec2,ec3,shor1995scheme,gottesman2009introduction,nielsen2002quantum,gottesman1997stabilizer .}
elements.sent-2411-130: Roughly speaking, stabilizer codes are the quantum analogue of linear codes for classical computing.
elements.sent-2411-131: More precisely, a $\llbracket n, k\rrbracket$ stabilizer code encodes $k$ logical qubits to $n$ physical qubits by embedding $2^k$ logical degrees of freedom into a subspace defined as the simultaneous $+1$ eigenspace of $n-k$ $n$-qubit Pauli operators, known as the stabilizers.
elements.sent-2411-132: Stabilizers comprise a considerably large class of quantum codes, including all CSS codes~\cite{steane1996multiple,calderbank1996good .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Generally, a stabilizer code is specified by the stabilizer tableau, the $n-k$ Pauli operator strings which define the code space.
elements.sent-2411-134: This description is simple and elegant, and has led to remarkable results such as the Gottesman-Knill theorem~\cite{aaronson2004improved , which efficiently simulates any Clifford operation classically, and the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov bound~\cite{nielsen2002quantum , which proves by a probabilistic technique that asymptotically good stabilizer codes exist.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: At the same time, the stabilizer tableau representation is prohibitive in many other ways.
elements.sent-2411-136: Without further structure, it is unclear as to how one might construct desirable codes by simply writing down a clever collection of $n-k$ Pauli strings, or how one might devise and analyze coding algorithms for a given tableau.
elements.sent-2411-137: For this reason, much of the constructive effort in quantum coding theory has relied on developing sophisticated CSS-type product operations that generate CSS codes from nice classical codes, and relying on classical algorithm analysis for inspiration~\cite{tillich2013quantum,breuckmann2021balanced,panteleev2021degenerate,calderbank1996good .}
elements.sent-2411-138: In this paper, we argue that a different approach based on graphs to represent stabilizer codes may also prove useful for the development and analysis of quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-139: The employment of graphs in code construction has a rich history in coding theory.
elements.sent-2411-140: Classically, Tanner graphs, which encode a classical linear code into a bipartite graph, built the bridge between expander graphs and classical codes and significantly contributed to modern classical coding~\cite{sipser1996expander .}
elements.sent-2411-141: In the quantum realm, graph reasoning---specifically the topological structure of Euclidean lattices with periodic boundary conditions---motivated the development of the toric and other surface codes~\cite{kitaev2003fault,bravyi2024high .}
elements.sent-2411-142: The toric code was even shown to be efficiently decoded by way of classical graph algorithms, in particular Edmonds' minimum weight perfect matching~\cite{edmonds1965maximum,edmonds1965paths .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Outside of quantum coding, tensor networks have played a key role in quantum simulation and algorithm design.
elements.sent-2411-144: The critical contributions of graphs in both classical and quantum coding in many specific constructive cases naturally lead to the questions of (a) what the most general graph techniques on stabilizer codes are and (b) how useful such a representation is for a variety of important coding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-145: This work takes a first step in answering both such questions.
elements.sent-2411-146: Our first contribution, in the direction of the first question, is the development of a simple graph representation of all stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-147: The general structure of the graph is known as an encoder-respecting form, which has the structure of a semi-bipartite graph that maps $k$ input-representing nodes to $n$ output-representing nodes.
elements.sent-2411-148: By semi-bipartite, we mean that inputs may not be connected but outputs generically may.
elements.sent-2411-149: Informally, our result may be stated as the following.
elements.sent-2411-150: There is an efficient compilation algorithm that maps tableaus to graphs in $O(n^3)$ time and vice versa in $O(n^2)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-151: The precise version of Theorem~\ref{thm:informal is given in Sections~\ref{sec:zxcf and \ref{sec:inversion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: This result establishes that our graph representation is sufficiently general to universally represent all stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-153: We devised the compilation algorithm from tableaus to graphs in part for the study of graph properties of well-known codes.
elements.sent-2411-154: As examples, we compile the famous 5-qubit, 7-qubit, and 9-qubit quantum codes and show that each take simple geometric forms respectively of a cone, cube, and tree-star.
elements.sent-2411-155: Conversely, Theorem~\ref{thm:informal enables us to essentially transform any semi-bipartite graph into a stabilizer code, in such a way that (a) any stabilizer code can be constructed this way and (b) the properties of the code are tied to the properties of the graph.}
elements.sent-2411-156: As a consequence, we can leverage the tools of graph theory and graph algorithms to study stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-157: We show that the key properties of a stabilizer code are controlled in a unified way, namely by the corresponding graph's degree.
elements.sent-2411-158: The distance of $C(G)$ is bounded above by the min-degree of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-159: There stabilizer tableau produced by the algorithm in Theorem~\ref{thm:informal has weights bounded by a quadratic function of the degree of certain nodes in $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-160: There exists an efficient algorithm which produces an encoding circuit for $C(G)$ whose depth is bounded by (up to a small additive constant) twice the maximum degree of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-161: Every constant-depth diagonal gate and the $\sqrt{X}$ can be implemented logically in $C(G)$ with a circuit of depth, respectively, about (up to an additive constant) twice the max-degree and the max-degree.
elements.sent-2411-162: The formal version of these statements are given in Section~\ref{sec:inversion .}
elements.sent-2411-163: To further strengthen the connection between graphs and codes, we next define a simple, one-player optimization-type game on a graph which we call quantum lights out (QLO), in Section~\ref{sec:inversion .}
elements.sent-2411-164: We prove that QLO unifies many important tasks in quantum coding, which opens a path towards the study of coding algorithms via their correspondence to approximately optimal QLO strategies.
elements.sent-2411-165: The above results demonstrate that graphs in many senses unify various aspects of stabilizer coding in a simple manner.
elements.sent-2411-166: Overall, we give four pieces of evidence that graphs serve as a useful representation.
elements.sent-2411-167: In the first, which we discussed above, we show that graphs enable efficient algorithms which produce encoding and logical-operator circuits whose depth are controlled by the degree.
elements.sent-2411-168: Later on, when we study decoding, we are able to bound the depths directly by the distance of the code for graphs which satisfy a certain property.
elements.sent-2411-169: In the second aspect, we explore the geometric and topological properties of graph as tools for building codes.
elements.sent-2411-170: Noticing that simple geometric solids realize celebrated quantum codes, we construct codes based on other such solids, including platonic solids and topological transformations of them.
elements.sent-2411-171: For simpler constructions, we are able to calculate the distance by direct computation.
elements.sent-2411-172: As an example, we produce a non-CSS stabilizer code from a dodecahedron, which has parameters $[[16, 4, 3]]$.
elements.sent-2411-173: We also construct a $[[65, 6, 5]]$ code by lifting the icosahedron to a covering space.
elements.sent-2411-174: For more complicated constructions, we use more indirect analytical techniques based on decoding.
elements.sent-2411-175: The main advantage of graphs as a constructive tool is their flexibility: we show that graphs enable us to more easily produce codes that have a desired numerical distance, by finding graphs which have a similar degree.
elements.sent-2411-176: Graphs also can be chosen to immediately satisfy desired experimental constraints based on locality, geometric structure, etc. Thus, the graph representation may prove helpful tools for code construction at constant-size scales fit for experimental use.
elements.sent-2411-178: In general, we believe that the graph representation serves as an excellent guide for the design of decoding algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-179: Historical evidence of minimum-weight perfect matching on the surface code suggests that graphs are indeed helpful.
elements.sent-2411-180: In this paper, we take a first step in that direction by designing a decoder that employs a simple greedy strategy on the QLO game.
elements.sent-2411-181: We give a general condition on graphs, which we call its sensitivity, that sufficiently characterizes a graph's amenity to be decoded well greedily.
elements.sent-2411-182: For concreteness, we also design a simple family of $[[\frac{m 2^m {m+1 , \frac{2^m {m+1 ]]$ codes, which we call the hypercube codes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Their distance is provably at most $m$, but we show that the decoder can correct at least $m/4$ errors on it, so that the distance is also at least $m/2$ (up to a $\pm 1$ additive constant).
elements.sent-2411-184: We conjecture, however, that a more careful analysis can prove that the distance is actually $m$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Assuming this conjecture, a small case of the hypercube code family is $[[112, 16, 7]]$.
elements.sent-2411-186: Moreover, our results imply a lower bound on the distance in terms of the degree, which in turn lead to bounds on encoding circuit depth, logical operator circuit depth, and stabilizer weight entirely in terms of distance.
elements.sent-2411-187: Finally, we exploit the structure of graphs to give a more fine-grained argument about random codes.
elements.sent-2411-188: By randomly connecting edges of our graphs, we give a large class of codes for which almost all members satisfy a tradeoff between rate, distance, and stabilizer weight.
elements.sent-2411-189: This extends the result of the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov bound, which shows by a coarse-grained probabilistic stabilizer tableau analysis a similar result with a rate-distance tradeoff but always large stabilizer weight.
elements.sent-2411-190: Such a result further exemplifies that graphs enable greater flexibility in code analysis.
elements.sent-2411-191: Our paper argues at a very general scope, namely at the level of all stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-192: The universality of our work therefore has limitations that do not exist for highly specific quantum low-density parity check (qLDPC) constructions.
elements.sent-2411-193: In particular, even when optimized as much as possible with known techniques, code properties such as check weight, encoding circuit depth, and logical operator circuit depth, will scale with the degree and therefore the code parameters, though not necessarily by a great deal (logarithmic, for example).
elements.sent-2411-194: Thus, our intention is not to use the graph representation as a means for continued study into asymptotically good qLDPC codes with very large constants in the manner of Refs.~~\cite{good1,good2,good3,panteleev2022asymptotically .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Rather, we consider our work to be complementary to qLDPC in the sense that we give general insight into stabilizer codes and new flexible ways to construct codes that may be tailored specifically for a given experimental implementation.
elements.sent-2411-196: As an example, a future quantum long-term memory device may seek a particular large numerical distance threshold but care little for comparably large check weights if the measurement error is sufficiently small.
elements.sent-2411-197: This philosophy underpins our explicit construction of codes which have desirable properties at small scales.
elements.sent-2411-198: We believe also that further applications of graph theoretical techniques may further improve the techniques in this paper to build increasingly practical recipes for code constructions that flexibly adapt to experimental constraints.
elements.sent-2411-199: More generally, we argue that graph theory provides a promising path forward generally across avenues of quantum coding theory.
elements.sent-2411-200: Recent breakthroughs in qLDPC codes, for example, have relied on graphs in their construction to generalize the use of surface code lattices~\cite{bravyi2024high .}
elements.sent-2411-201: Perhaps further generalizations of these specific types of constructive techniques, as well as less universal but more fine-grained (i.e. finding certain graph constructions which map to codes in such a way that some properties scale favorably or not at all with parameters) refinements of the universal graph representation will lead to new constructions of and insights into quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-202: Graph forms have been exploited in many ways in the quantum computation literature, most generally via tensor network representations.
elements.sent-2411-203: One simple case of a graph representation is a graph state.
elements.sent-2411-204: Our reduction from tableau to graph utilizes insights from \citet{hu2022improved , and in the special case of $n = k$ in our work, there is a transformation that reduces our tableau-to-graph map to their corresponding map.}
elements.sent-2411-205: We remark that \citet{mcelvanney2022complete , building upon \citet{hu2022improved , derived a different unique canonical form for stabilizer states that are particularly convenient for measurement-based quantum computation.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Independently, \citet{yu2007graphical also utilized graph theory in quantum coding, albeit at a much more general level (including non-stabilizer codes) for constructive and classification purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Parts of our work---particularly the reduction from tableau to graph---utilize rules and notation from the ZX calculus.
elements.sent-2411-208: Recent works have also explored the use of ZX calculus to diagrammatically express quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-209: A recent work of \citet{kissinger2022phase developed independently of this work also constructs diagrammatic forms for CSS codes.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Their forms are substantially different for ours and serve a different purpose.
elements.sent-2411-211: Primarily, Kissinger establishes a correspondence between CSS codes and ZX diagrams, by non-uniquely mapping CSS codes to such diagrams and mapping a subset of the ZX diagrams (the phase-free ones) to CSS codes.
elements.sent-2411-212: The tableau-to-graph portion of our work extends the conceptual idea of diagrammatic forms inspired by ZX calculus to all stabilizer codes, but takes a significantly different approach and utilizes the result for constructive and algorithmic purposes.
elements.sent-2411-213: More generally, ZX calculus approaches to quantum error correction have been considered in various ways.
elements.sent-2411-214: Two relatively recent works which utilize the ZX calculus exemplify these different approaches.
elements.sent-2411-215: In the first way, \citet{wu2023zx represent specifically graph codes with ZX calculus diagrams.}
elements.sent-2411-216: This approach, while not general to stabilizer or CSS codes, has shown promise in code analysis.
elements.sent-2411-217: The second, due to ~\citet{chancellor2016graphical , uses the ZX calculus specifically to quantize classical codes in the spirit of CSS code construction.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Both of these works give evidence that graphs enable new insights into quantum coding, both in their construction and analysis, and thus motivate our completely general theory of graph representations.
elements.sent-2411-219: Moreover, aside from our tableau-to-graph reduction algorithm which does use elements of the ZX calculus, our work builds everything essentially only from the standard definition graphs.
elements.sent-2411-220: We emphasize that the graphical diagrams of the ZX calculus require additional structure.
elements.sent-2411-221: Therefore, graph representations further simplify related previous works that also devise similar representations based on the ZX calculus.
elements.sent-2411-222: The specific motivations, applications, and analysis in these papers are also distinct from those in this work.
elements.sent-2411-223: Building upon a preliminary earlier version of this work, \citet{khesin2024equivalence conducted further analysis of graph representations in the special case of CSS codes, wherein the graphs become bipartite.}
elements.sent-2411-224: They specifically analyzed different ways to further simplify the graph representation and potential advantages in doing so.
elements.sent-2411-225: From Tableaus to Graphs We begin by constructing the general graph form and compiling any stabilizer tableau into such a graph.
elements.sent-2411-226: More precisely, we will compile an equivalence class of encoding Clifford circuits corresponding to an equivalence class of stabilizer tableaus, and at the end of this section we discuss how to compile starting with a tableau~\cite{gottesman2009introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-227: As a starting point, we appeal to the ZX-calculus, a graph language for vectors that has become of great interest in quantum information research~\cite{coecke2008interacting,zx1,zx2,zx3,zx4,kissinger2022phase .}
elements.sent-2411-228: The ZX-calculus produces visual diagrams that represent quantum states, circuits, and more.
elements.sent-2411-229: As with any formal logical system, the ZX calculus has a set of rules which may be iteratively applied to transform diagrams into equivalent diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-230: These rules are representations of identities in quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-231: The ZX-calculus has become of more interest than ever in fault-tolerant quantum computation and quantum compiler theory because it can explicitly visualize quantum properties such as entanglement in an intuitive manner.
elements.sent-2411-232: It has recently been applied to a host of quantum computation problems, including lattice surgery~\cite{de2020zx and quantum optimization~\cite{kissinger2020reducing .}}
elements.sent-2411-233: Importantly, the ZX-calculus is, for stabilizer tableaus, complete (equalities of tableaus can be derived from corresponding ZX diagrams), sound (vice versa), and universal (every quantum operation can be expressed in the ZX-calculus)~\cite{coecke2008interacting,backens2014zx .}
elements.sent-2411-234: We are consequently motivated to leverage the ZX calculus as an intermediate step in the development of a graph presentation.
elements.sent-2411-235: Ultimately, our graphs will be simple graphs and not the more complicated and structured diagrams in the ZX calculus.
elements.sent-2411-236: The diagrams that we map our circuits into are a subset of ZX diagrams that are equivalent by a simple transformation to graphs.
elements.sent-2411-237: To more clearly elucidate the transformation from tableau to graph, we construct the map from a composition of simpler maps.
elements.sent-2411-238: In particular, we first convert the tableau into a circuit.
elements.sent-2411-239: Next we show that up to an equivalence relation, we can efficiently biject the circuits into a class of ZX diagrams of a certain form that satisfies four rules.
elements.sent-2411-240: We thus denote these diagrams ZX canonical forms (ZXCFs).
elements.sent-2411-241: Lastly, we remove unnecessary structure from ZXCFs to obtain a graph representation of the code.
elements.sent-2411-242: This is pictorially represented by the following maps.. \begin{equation}\label{eq:map_sequence} Tableau \longleftrightarrow Encoder \longleftrightarrow ZXCF \stackrel{LE}{\longleftrightarrow} Graph .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-243: In absence of the last step, everything is completely invertible and has an interpretation as a unique compiler.
elements.sent-2411-244: For the last step, the map between ZXCFs and graphs is invertible modulo local equivalence; that is, modulo local (1-qubit) Clifford operations on the outputs.
elements.sent-2411-245: An immediate consequence for this ZXCF compiler is a diagrammatic method of testing equality between stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-246: We begin the formalism by noting that properties of a code depend only on the codespace, and thus there is a unitary degree of freedom on the logical space.
elements.sent-2411-247: In particular, encoding circuits for a given stabilizer tableau will produce the same stabilizer code if and only if they differ by an image.
elements.sent-2411-248: We therefore make the following definition.
elements.sent-2411-249: Two circuits are equivalent as Clifford encoders if they have the same image over all possible input states.
elements.sent-2411-250: Equivalently, $\CC_1$ and $\CC_2$ are equivalent encoders if there exists a unitary $U$ such that $\CC_1 = \CC_2 U$ or $\CC_2 = \CC_1 U$.
elements.sent-2411-251: Any reference we make to encoding circuits will either implicitly or explicitly refer to them up to this equivalence, and indeed they all map to the same stabilizer code.
elements.sent-2411-252: ZX Canonical Form Construction In the intermediate steps utilizing ZX calculus rules, we follow the standard notation of ZX-calculus graph diagrammatics, specified in~\citet{backens2014zx ; we refer the reader there for the basics of the ZX calculus and transformation rules.}
elements.sent-2411-253: A ZX diagram is a graph with additional structure.
elements.sent-2411-254: In particular, nodes may be either red or green, nodes may be decorated with local Clifford operators, and edges may be Hadamarded.
elements.sent-2411-255: Additionally, nodes may have a ``free edge'', an edge connected only to one node and dangling on the other side.
elements.sent-2411-256: Green nodes are associated with $Z$ operators, and red with $X$.
elements.sent-2411-257: Each node may be associated with a local Clifford operator (which may be expressed as a phase which is a multiple of $\pi/2$), and each edge may have a Hadamard gate on it.
elements.sent-2411-258: We colour an edge blue if it has a Hadamard gate on it~\cite{duncan2020graph (some papers use instead a line with a box, e.g.~\cite{backens2014zx ).}}
elements.sent-2411-259: The circuit takes $n - k$ input qubits to $n$ output qubits, for $k \geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-260: The specific structure of our graphs will be those in the following form.
elements.sent-2411-261: An encoder-respecting form $\mathcal{D}$ has only green ($Z$) nodes, and is structured as a semi-bipartite graph.
elements.sent-2411-262: A semi-bipartite graph has a left and right cluster, such that left-cluster nodes may have edges only to right-cluster nodes, but right-cluster nodes have no such restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-263: In our case, the left and right cluster are denoted as the input and output clusters, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-264: The input cluster $\CI$ has $k$ nodes associated with the $k$ input qubits of the corresponding encoder, and the output cluster $\widetilde{\CO}$ has $n$ nodes.
elements.sent-2411-265: Each input node has a free (not connected to any other nodes) edge---the input edge.
elements.sent-2411-266: Similarly, each output node has a free output edge.
elements.sent-2411-267: The output edges are numbered from $1$ to $n$---in order from left to right on an incomplete stabilizer tableau or top to bottom on Clifford circuit output wires.
elements.sent-2411-268: For convenience, we refer to the node $v$ connected to an output edge numbered $i$ as the output node numbered $i$.
elements.sent-2411-269: The design of the encoder-respecting form graphically illustrates how information propagates from input to output (which edges connect $\CI$ to $\widetilde{\CO}$) as well as the entanglement structure (which edges connect $\widetilde{\CO}$ to $\widetilde{\CO}$) of the underlying encoder.
elements.sent-2411-270: We emphasize that this idea is only for intuition, as there exists equivalence transformation rules on ZX diagrams that appear to change the connectivity in nontrivial ways but are nonetheless the same diagram.
elements.sent-2411-271: Note that the structure of $\mathcal{D}$ gives rise to a natural binary ``partial adjacency matrix'' $M_{\mathcal{D }$ of size $k \times n$, which describes the edges between $\CI$ and $\CO$ akin to the standard graph adjacency matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Although this structure appears intuitively appealing, it is insufficient because there exists several equivalence transformations in the ZX calculus that yield an equivalent (but not obviously so) diagram.
elements.sent-2411-273: We therefore constrain an encoder-respecting form into a ZXCF via four additional rules.
elements.sent-2411-274: For notational convenience, we will interchangeably refer to applying an operation on a node's free edge as applying an operation on the node.
elements.sent-2411-275: Edge Rule: The ZXCF must have exactly one $Z$-node per free edge and every internal edge must have a Hadamard on it.
elements.sent-2411-276: Hadamard Rule: No output node $v$ may both have a Hadamard gate on its output edge and have edges connecting $v$ to lower-numbered nodes or input nodes.
elements.sent-2411-277: RREF Rule: $M_{\mathcal{D }$ must be in reduced row-echelon form (RREF).}
elements.sent-2411-278: Clifford Rule: Let $\CP \seq \CO$ be the nodes associated with the pivot columns of the RREF matrix $M_{\mathcal{D }$, so that $|\CP| = n - k$.}
elements.sent-2411-279: The only local Cliffords allowed on nodes are $\set{I, S, Z, SZ, H, HZ}$, but there can be no operations at all on input or pivot nodes.
elements.sent-2411-280: There can be no input-input edges or pivot-pivot edges.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{example_violations.png} \caption{Example of an encoder-respecting form and some ways it might violate the 4 rules.} \label{fog:example_violations}
elements.sent-2411-281: We have elected for emphasis to include the $Z$-nodes only constraint in the edge rule and the no input-input edges constraint in the Clifford rule even though these are redundant with that of the encoder-respecting form.
elements.sent-2411-282: To provide some visual intuition on these 4 rules, Fig.~\ref{fog:example_violations depicts a generic example of some possible violations to the rules.}
elements.sent-2411-283: We define the four rules such that the ZXCF is homogeneous in node type and minimal in terms of decorated operators, number of edges.
elements.sent-2411-284: It can be shown~\cite{van2020zx,backens2014zx that each of these rules corresponds to certain equivalence transformations.}
elements.sent-2411-285: For example, row operations on $M_{\mathcal{D }$ correspond to unitaries on the input.}
elements.sent-2411-286: Rather than give all the rules, we will directly devise an encoder to ZXCF map and prove that such a map is efficient and bijective.
elements.sent-2411-287: Any Clifford encoder has a unique equivalent ZXCF satisfying the Edge, Hadamard, RREF, and Clifford rules.
elements.sent-2411-288: There exists an algorithm, running in worst-case $O(n^3)$ time, that on input a description of the Clifford encoder outputs a corresponding ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-290: We briefly sketch the proof idea of Theorem~\ref{thm:canonicity but defer the details to Appendix~\ref{app:sec:compiler .}}
elements.sent-2411-291: The proof involves a series of transformations via ZX equivalence rules.
elements.sent-2411-292: In the first step, we apply an isomorphism between circuits and states, and proceed to apply a result of \citet{hu2022improved to preliminarily represent the circuit as the ZX diagram of a corresponding state.}
elements.sent-2411-293: We then transform the diagram into one representing a circuit, and carefully choose a series of equivalence transformations that iteratively force the diagram to satisfy a rule above without at the same time violating a different rule that has previously been satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-294: The resulting product is precisely a ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-295: In fact, because the steps are equivalence transformations, distinct encoders map to distinct ZXCFs.
elements.sent-2411-296: The only remaining aspect of the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:canonicity is whether this one-to-one map is bijective.}
elements.sent-2411-297: We prove this claim in Section~\ref{sec:compiler .}
elements.sent-2411-298: There are numerous algorithms for mapping a stabilizer tableau into a Clifford encoding circuit~\cite{nielsen2002quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-299: If we quotient out operations which leave the code space invariant, then any such map is also bijective.
elements.sent-2411-301: For a $(n-k) \times n$ tableau, begin by drawing $n$ output wires.
elements.sent-2411-302: At each step, we first simplify the tableau by applying a Clifford operation, and then measure out one of the qubits to remove one row and column from the tableau.
elements.sent-2411-303: Repeating inductively yields a Clifford circuit that takes $k$ qubits to $n$ qubits.
elements.sent-2411-304: The procedure at each step begins by finding a Clifford operation $U$ that turns the first stabilizer into $Z_1=Z\otimes I\otimes\dots\otimes I$, in the manner dictated by the Gottesman-Knill theorem.
elements.sent-2411-305: We then multiply the remaining rows by the first until the entire first column of the tableau has only $I$, with the exception of the first row.
elements.sent-2411-306: This can always be done, since the $n$-qubit Pauli operators in each row of the matrix must commute.
elements.sent-2411-307: We then post-select on the $+1$ result of a computational basis measurement by applying $\bra0$ in the reverse direction.
elements.sent-2411-308: When reading the circuit in the forward direction, this is equivalent to initializing an ancillary qubit in the $\ket0$ state.
elements.sent-2411-309: The effect of $U$ and the measurement is equivalent to applying $\frac{I+P 2$, where $P$ is the first row of the stabilizer tableau.}
elements.sent-2411-310: This is equivalent to post-selecting on the $+1$ measurement result of that stabilizer.
elements.sent-2411-311: Having measured the qubit, we remove its corresponding row and column from the tableau, and repeat on a tableau of $n-1$ qubits and $n-k-1$ rows, until there are no rows left, at which point the remaining $k$ wires that have not been measured out become the input qubits in the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-312: We conclude by drawing $k$ input wires for those qubits.
elements.sent-2411-313: Proof of Canonicity We next prove that our proposed ZXCF is indeed canonical.
elements.sent-2411-314: In other words, two equivalent stabilizer tableaus---generators of the same subspace of the $n$-qubit Hilbert space---will map to the same ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-315: We proceed by counting the number of stabilizer tableaus and ZXCFs.
elements.sent-2411-316: For notational convenience, solely in this section, we will let $k$ be the number of rows of the tableau rather than the number of inputs; that is, let $k \leftrightarrow n-k$.
elements.sent-2411-317: First, the number of stabilizer tableaus with $k$ stabilizers on $n$ qubits is \begin{equation}\frac{\prod\limits_{i=1}^k2\cdot4^n/2^{i-1}-2\cdot2^{i-1}}{\prod\limits_{i=1}^k2^k-2^{i-1}}=\prod_{i=1}^k\frac{2^{2n-i+2}-2^i}{2^k-2^{i-1}}. \label{eq:zx-count}\end{equation} For each row $i$, there are $2 \cdot 4^n$ possible Pauli strings (including the sign), but the requirement that they commute with previous rows divides the count by $2^{i-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-318: Of these $2 \cdot 4^n/2^{i-1}$ valid strings, $2^{i-1}$ strings are linear combinations of previous strings, with a factor of 2 for strings that differ by a sign from previous strings.
elements.sent-2411-319: We have overcounted, however, since there are many ways to find a set of stabilizer generators for a particular tableau.
elements.sent-2411-320: In particular, when choosing a set of generators for a Clifford encoder, we have $2^k-1$ choices for the first generator (subtracting the identity).
elements.sent-2411-321: There are then $2^k-2$ ways of choosing the next element, as we cannot pick anything in the span of the elements chosen so far.
elements.sent-2411-322: Repeating inductively gives the denominator above.
elements.sent-2411-323: Next, the number of ZXCF diagrams for the same $n$ and $k$ can be expressed by the following fourfold recursive function $f$ evaluated at $p = o = 0$. \begin{equation}f(n,k,p,o)=\begin{cases} 1 \text{ if } n=k=0 A_{n,k,p,o}+B_{n,k,p,o} \text{else} \end{cases} \label{eq:zxcf-count}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}A_{n,k,p,o}= 2^of(n-1,k,p+1,o)\end{equation} if $n \neq k$ and $A_{n,k,p,o =0$ if $n = k$, and where \begin{equation}B_{n,k,p,o}=(2^{2p+o+2}+2)f(n-1,k-1,p,o+1)\end{equation} if $k \neq 0$ and $B_{n,k,p,o =0$ if $k = 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-324: This function is computed with a base case of an empty tableau when $n=k=0$.
elements.sent-2411-325: Derivation of this function is left to Appendix~\ref{app:recursion .}
elements.sent-2411-326: We solve for an explicit form to obtain \begin{equation}f(n,k,p,o) = 2^{o (n - k)} \prod_{i=1}^k \frac{(2^{n+1} - 2^i) (2^{n - i + 1 + 2 p + o} + 1)}{2^{k} - 2^{i-1}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-327: One can verify by standard induction that $f(n,k,0,0)$ gives the same expression as Eq.~(\ref{eq:zx-count ), proving that ZXCF is indeed canonical.}
elements.sent-2411-328: In the final step, we reduce a ZXCF directly to a graph.
elements.sent-2411-329: We remark that any local Clifford operation must, by the Clifford rule, be placed only on non-pivot output nodes.
elements.sent-2411-330: However, local Cliffords do not change the weights of the set of correctable errors.
elements.sent-2411-331: Thus, local Cliffords do not change the code distance or any other code parameters.
elements.sent-2411-332: If we also quotient out this equivalence, we may remove all local Cliffords entirely.
elements.sent-2411-333: Since all internal edges have Hadamards, we can remove those as well and simply remember that they exist for inversion.
elements.sent-2411-334: Similarly, we can remove all free edges.
elements.sent-2411-335: The remaining diagram is identically a graph, specified entirely by a collection of nodes and edges.
elements.sent-2411-336: As a consequence, we have completed the map sequence from Eqn.~(\ref{eq:map_sequence ), except for the graph to ZXCF map.}
elements.sent-2411-337: For any stabilizer code, there exists at least one equivalent code that has a presentation as a semi-bipartite graph with no local Clifford operations.
elements.sent-2411-338: Moreover, we make the following observation.
elements.sent-2411-339: Application to Quantum Codes It is illuminating to observe the graph representation of well-known quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-340: Not only does it inform of geometric structure embedded in the code, but it also provides a guide as to how such codes may be generalized.
elements.sent-2411-341: We provide three examples for study.
elements.sent-2411-342: Consider first the nine-qubit code, due to \citet{shor1995scheme , which uses 9 physical qubits to encode 1 logical qubit.}
elements.sent-2411-343: The Shor code may be represented by the stabilizer tableau \begin{equation} Z Z I I I I I I I Z I Z I I I I I I I I I Z Z I I I I I I I Z I Z I I I I I I I I I Z Z I I I I I I I Z I Z X X X X X X I I I X X X I I I X X X  .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} Z Z I I I I I I I Z I Z I I I I I I I I I Z Z I I I I I I I Z I Z I I I I I I I I I Z Z I I I I I I I Z I Z X X X X X X I I I X X X I I I X X X  .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-344: In Fig.~\ref{fig:nine-qubit-code , we give the graph representation in (a) and the ZXCF in (b).}
elements.sent-2411-345: We denote in the ZXCF the input node with I, and the free edges are dotted for emphasis.
elements.sent-2411-346: In the graph presentation, the input node is blue and the outputs are black.
elements.sent-2411-348: There are three identical sectors of the outputs, two with Hadamarded outputs and one without.
elements.sent-2411-349: This resembles our expectations from examination of the un-normalized qubit representation of the Shor code, $(\ket{000 \pm \ket{111 )^{\otimes 3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-350: The graph, on the other hand, is a star-shaped tree.
elements.sent-2411-351: It is easy to imagine how one might extend the Shor code into an infinite family of graphs, namely by recursively giving every outer (leaf) node two children.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{9-qubit-code.png} \caption{(a) Graph presentation and (b) ZXCF of the 9-qubit code. The input in the graph is shown in blue. Note that the graph encoder uses a slightly different basis as since its output edges do not contain Hadamard gates.} \label{fig:nine-qubit-code}
elements.sent-2411-352: Next, we compile Steane's seven-qubit code, a central construction in many fault-tolerant quantum computation schemes~\cite{steane1996multiple .}
elements.sent-2411-353: The code is represented by the following tableau: \begin{equation} I I I X X X X I X X I I X X X I X I X I X I I I Z Z Z Z I Z Z I I Z Z Z I Z I Z I Z  .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation} I I I X X X X I X X I I X X X I X I X I X I I I Z Z Z Z I Z Z I I Z Z Z I Z I Z I Z  .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-354: In our ZXCF, this code takes the form given in Fig.~\ref{fig:7-qubit-code .}
elements.sent-2411-355: In particular, the nodes of the diagram are simply the corners of a cube.
elements.sent-2411-356: A similar picture has been given in a different context in~\citet{duncan2013verifying .}
elements.sent-2411-357: The Steane code's generalization is also evident, via extensions into hypercubes of arbitrary dimension.
elements.sent-2411-358: We explore precisely such a coding scheme in Section~\ref{subsec:decoder .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale=0.475]{7-qubit-code.png} \caption{(a) Graph presentation and (b) ZXCF of the 7-qubit code. The input in the graph is shown in blue. Note that the graph encoder uses a slightly different basis as since its output edges do not contain Hadamard gates.} \label{fig:7-qubit-code}
elements.sent-2411-359: We observe that the stabilizers of the 7-qubit code are positioned at exactly the $1$-indices in the binary representation of a node label.
elements.sent-2411-360: We can see elegant symmetries in Fig.~\ref{fig:7-qubit-code with the positions of the nodes and their expressions in binary.}
elements.sent-2411-361: The reason why the nodes adjacent to 0 are not 1, 2, and 4, (respectively 001, 010, and 100 in binary) is because the rules of the ZXCF require that the Hadamarded output edges not be connected to lower-numbered nodes.
elements.sent-2411-362: As a final example we consider the five-qubit code, which is the smallest code one can achieve for correction of an arbitrary single-qubit error and has been studied experimentally~\cite{gottesman2009introduction,knill2001benchmarking .}
elements.sent-2411-363: Its tableau representation is given by \begin{equation} X Z Z X I I X Z Z X X I X Z Z Z X I X Z  .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation} X Z Z X I I X Z Z X X I X Z Z Z X I X Z  .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-364: Our procedure produces the ZXCF shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:5-qubit-code .}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[scale=0.475]{5-qubit-code.png} \caption{(a) Graph presentation and (b) ZXCF of the 5-qubit code. The input in the graph is shown in blue.} \label{fig:5-qubit-code}
elements.sent-2411-365: The 5-qubit code takes the shape of either a pentagon with an input in the middle or a cone, depending on how one sets up the graph.
elements.sent-2411-366: Consequently, a simple generalization is to use a $n$-gon, or equivalently add more nodes to the circle in the cone.
elements.sent-2411-367: Such elegant and simple structure in the 5, 7, and 9-qubit codes suggest that other graphs with similar elegant structure may yield interesting quantum codes, either of constant size or in family.
elements.sent-2411-368: We explore this idea in Section~\ref{subsec:smallcodes .}
elements.sent-2411-369: The inverse map: from graphs to stabilizer codes The bijective relation between stabilizer codes and graphs---particularly those in the ZX canonical form---give justification that graphs are at least as powerful as stabilizer tableaus for the expression of codes.
elements.sent-2411-370: In this section, we lay the foundation for the use of graphs as constructive tools for code design.
elements.sent-2411-371: Such a mapping from graphs to stabilizer tableaus can be viewed as an ``inverse map'' to the algorithm given in Section~\ref{sec:compiler , with some caveats relating to the fact that not all graphs can be equivalently represented by ZXCFs and therefore not all graphs can be mapped into codes.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Let $G$ be a graph with $n+k$ nodes, separated into $k$ input nodes $\CI$, a choice of $k$ pivot nodes $\CP$, and $n-k$ output nodes $\CO$.
elements.sent-2411-373: To ensure that our operations are well-defined, there must exist equivalence operations that map $G$ into a ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-374: The only nontrivial restriction this requirement poses is on the choice of pivots.
elements.sent-2411-375: The RREF rule ensures that every distinct pivot is connected to exactly one distinct input; in other words, $\CP$ and $\CI$ are perfectly matched.
elements.sent-2411-376: Because row operations on the partial adjacency matrix are equivalences, it suffices to choose pivots such that there exists a row-reduced partial adjacency matrix $M_{\mathcal{D }$ for which the chosen $k$ pivot nodes correspond to the $k$ pivot columns of $M_{\mathcal{D }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-377: A simple example of this subtlety is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:badpivot .}
elements.sent-2411-378: Here, it is impossible to choose the first two outputs to be the pivots because the third output is forced to be a pivot when we row-reduce.
elements.sent-2411-379: Note, however, that this subtle restriction poses no issue if every distinct pivot we choose is already connected to exactly one distinct input (as in the case in Section~\ref{subsec:randcodes ) or if we can observe directly that the pivots we have chosen do correspond to the pivot columns after row reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-380: Henceforth, we will denote $G$ as a graph for which a set of valid pivots $\CP$ have been specified alongside a set of input nodes $\CI$.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{badpivot.png} \caption{A simple example for which the third output node is necessarily a pivot. The inputs are blue and outputs are black. The example is given on the left, and an equivalent graph is given on the right.} \label{fig:badpivot}
elements.sent-2411-381: Assuming the choice of $\CP$ is valid, $G$ will be equivalent to a ZXCF by a series of equivalence transformations.
elements.sent-2411-382: First, we place a free edge on every node and place a Hadamard on all internal edges, satisfying the edge rule.
elements.sent-2411-383: The Hadamard rule is vacuously satisfied, as we will not place any Hadamard on output nodes.
elements.sent-2411-384: We can row-reduce the partial adjacency matrix to satisfy the RREF rule.
elements.sent-2411-385: Next, we strip input-input edges, which are unitaries on the inputs and thus represent equivalent encoders.
elements.sent-2411-386: Lastly, as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:zxcf , there is an equivalence relation that strips pivot-pivot edges.}
elements.sent-2411-387: This procedure results in a ZXCF with no local Clifford on nodes at all.
elements.sent-2411-388: While there exist encoders that can only be expressed with local Clifford operations that this map cannot produce, the map can always produce a locally equivalent encoder, and is otherwise completely general.
elements.sent-2411-389: In practice, we never transform our graphs into a ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-390: The only transformation we shall make is endow every node with a free edge.
elements.sent-2411-391: This endowment enables a simpler description of operator actions on the graph.
elements.sent-2411-392: However, in our diagrams we will omit the free edges to reduce clutter.
elements.sent-2411-393: We can also strip input-input edges and pivot-pivot edges if desired for convenience.
elements.sent-2411-394: Aside from that, knowing that the graph is equivalent to a ZXCF, we can write down the stabilizer generators of its corresponding ZXCF directly.
elements.sent-2411-395: To do so, we first establish notation.
elements.sent-2411-396: Let $\D_{i=1 ^n A_i = A_1 \,\D\, \cdots \,\D\, A_n$ for sets $A_1, \hdots, A_n$, where $\D$ is the symmetric difference.}
elements.sent-2411-397: The symmetric difference is both commutative and associative, so this iterative procedure is well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-399: We define $i(v) = \set{w \in \CI \,|\, w \sim v}$ the inputs connected to $v$ and $p(v) =\set{w \in \CP \,|\, w \sim v}$ the pivots connected to $v$, which each consist of only one vertex if $v$ is a pivot or an input, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-400: For later use we similarly define $o(v)=\set{w \in \CO \,|\, w \sim v}$, the set of non-pivot outputs connected to $v$.
elements.sent-2411-401: We also define $N_o(v) = \set{w \in \CO \cup \CP \,|\, w \sim v =o(v)\cup p(v)$ the non-input neighbours of $v \in \CO$, and more generally $N_o(A) = \D_{v \in A N_o(v)$ and similarly for $i(A)$, $p(A)$, and $o(A)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-402: The symmetric difference is useful for correctly counting the parities of Pauli operations applied to certain nodes, i.e. if a Pauli is applied twice in the set, it is effectively not applied at all.
elements.sent-2411-403: Let $X(w)$ be a Pauli on (the free edge of) node $w$, and $X(A) = \bigotimes_{v \in A X(v)$; $Z$ operators are defined analogously.}
elements.sent-2411-404: Note that we may only directly apply operators to (the free edge of) output nodes because these are physical operators.
elements.sent-2411-405: With this notation, we define the following map $\CS : G \mapsto S$ from graph to stabilizer tableau, the output of which we call the canonical stabilizers of the code represented by $G$.. \begin{equation}\label{eq:inverse} \CS := \set{X(v) Z(N_o(v)) X(p(i(v))) Z(N_o(p(i(v))))}_{v \in \CO} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-407: These stabilizers are readily seen to be a valid generating set.
elements.sent-2411-408: There are exactly $n-k$ generators, and all are independent because only $S_v$ has a $X$ on $v$.
elements.sent-2411-409: Every pair of stabilizers $S_v$ and $S_w$ also have even $X$-$Z$ overlap--and thus commute---because the graphs are undirected.
elements.sent-2411-410: In principle, one could disregard the entirety of the previous section, define Eqn.~(\ref{eq:inverse ) as a map from graphs to stabilizers, and explore their properties.}
elements.sent-2411-411: However, it is unclear a priori from this approach how expressive such a class of stabilizers would be and the motivation behind such an assertion would be obscure.
elements.sent-2411-412: We next show that such a map generates precisely the stabilizer group of the encoder that the corresponding ZXCF of $G$ represents.
elements.sent-2411-413: Because graphs do not have local Clifford operations associated to them, we can treat $\CS$ as the inverse map from graph to ZXCF, modulo local Cliffords, i.e. up to local equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-414: To do so, we appeal to the two most fundamental equivalence rules of the ZX calculus.
elements.sent-2411-415: Indeed, we will use these rules many times in the course of our calculations.
elements.sent-2411-416: Operators may be placed on edges and associated with a on either side of the edge.
elements.sent-2411-417: If an operator placed on an internal edge between $v$ and $w$ is associated with $v$, it can be equivalently associated with $w$ instead.
elements.sent-2411-418: However, if the internal edge is Hadamarded, the operator is flipped $X \leftrightarrow Z$ during this change of association.
elements.sent-2411-419: An operator placed on a free edge of $v$ is vacuously associated with $v$.
elements.sent-2411-420: If a $X$ operator is placed on an edge associated with $v$, it can be equivalently commuted through $v$ into $X$'s on every other edge (including the free edge) connected to $v$, each remaining associated with $v$.
elements.sent-2411-421: If a $Z$ operator is placed on an edge associated to $v$, it may be equivalently commuted through as a $Z$ on any one other edge associated to $v$, remaining associated to $v$.
elements.sent-2411-422: The rules provided in Lemma~\ref{lemma:rules give a prescription for generating stabilizers.}
elements.sent-2411-424: First, place a $X$ on the free edge of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-425: By rule (2), this $X$ equivalently transforms into $X$ on all other edges connected to $v$, associating with $v$.
elements.sent-2411-426: For edges connecting $v$ to a non-input node $w$, we re-associate them by rule (1) to $w$, and since all internal edges are Hadamarded, these operators become $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-427: They commute through by rule (3) to the free edge of $w$ and remain there as $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-428: We perform a similar transformation except that we do not commute the $Z$'s to the free edges of inputs because these correspond to operations on inputs (i.e. logical operations) rather than operations on outputs (i.e. physical operations).
elements.sent-2411-429: Instead, we commute them through canonically to the pivot associated with that input, turning the $Z$ back into the $X$ as we re-associate the operator with the pivot.
elements.sent-2411-430: Finally, we commute the $X$ through the pivot.
elements.sent-2411-431: Since the $X$ was on an internal edge between the input and pivot, a $X$ appears on the free edge of the pivot.
elements.sent-2411-432: The remaining $X$'s become $Z$'s associated with the output neighbours of the pivot and commute through to their free edges by rule (3).
elements.sent-2411-433: To reverse all these operations and achieve the identity, thereby creating a stabilizer, we apply all these operations in reverse, precisely $X(v) Z(N_o(v)) X(p(i(v))) Z(N_o(p(i(v))))$.
elements.sent-2411-434: Therefore, we have proven the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-435: From here, we can also easily write down a canonical set of logical Pauli operators for a given graph, by utilizing the same two transformation rules from above.
elements.sent-2411-436: For each input node $v_i \in \CI$ corresponding to the $i^\text{th}$ logical qubit, construct $\overline{X _i$ by placing $X(v_i)$, which is by definition a logical $X$ on the $i^\text{th}$ logical qubit.}
elements.sent-2411-437: To obtain the physical operator, we commute $X(v_i)$ to the (free edges of the) output nodes---here $X(v_i)$ commutes through to become $Z(N_o(v_i))$.
elements.sent-2411-439: Similarly, $\overline{Z _i = X(p_i) Z(N_o(p_i))$, where $p_i$ is the pivot associated with $v_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-440: We place a $X$ on $p_i$ to canonically define logical $Z$, but any neighbour of $v_i$ would suffice.
elements.sent-2411-441: In this way, we have used the equivalence rules on every possible node: on $\CO$, they create stabilizers; on $\CI$, they create logical $X$; and on $\CP$, they create logical $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-442: As a consequence of our canonical logical operator construction, we observe that the degree of $G$ controls the upper bound of the distance as well as the stabilizer weights of $\CS(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-443: The distance of $\CS(G)$ is at most the minimum degree of any vertex in $\CI \cup N_o(\CI)$.
elements.sent-2411-444: For each $v\in\CI$, $X(v)$ is a logical operation which is equal to $Z(N_o(v))$.
elements.sent-2411-445: The weight of this operation is equal to the degree of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-446: Similarly, for each $v\in N_o(\CI)$, the rules of the graph tell us that applying $X(v)$ as well as applying $Z$ to all neighbours of $v$ does not change the circuit effected by the graph.
elements.sent-2411-447: Since $v$ neighbours at least one input node, this means that the logical operation formed by the Paulis applied to the inputs is equal to the Paulis applied to the outputs, including $v$.
elements.sent-2411-448: This size of this set is equal to $|N_o(v)|+1$, which is at most the degree of $v$, since $v$ neighbours an input.
elements.sent-2411-449: The construction of these logical operations proves the desired upper bound on the distance.
elements.sent-2411-450: This also shows that the statement of the corollary could be strengthened slightly by replacing the degree of input neighbours by the number of their output neighbours plus one.
elements.sent-2411-451: Given a graph $G$, define \begin{equation}\d^*_{\CO} = \max_{v \in \CO} \deg(v) ,\quad \d^*_{\CO \CI} = \max_{v \in \CO} i(v) ,\quad \d^*_{\CP \CO} = \max_{v \in \CP} N_o(v) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-452: Then $\max_{S \in \CS(G) |S| \leq 1 + \d^*_{\CO + \d^*_{\CO \CI \d^*_{\CP \CO}$, where $|S|$ is the weight of a stabilizer $S$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-453: This result is an immediate consequence of Eqn.~(\ref{eq:inverse ).}
elements.sent-2411-454: In the next section, we will show that another important property, encoding depth, is also bounded above by the degree.
elements.sent-2411-455: We make two remarks regarding the utility of such degree bounds.
elements.sent-2411-456: First, for a general stabilizer code it is unclear as to how one may give a nontrivial upper bound on properties like distance and circuit depth.
elements.sent-2411-457: The degree allows us to algorithmically do so by compiling a stabilizer tableau into a graph and then examining the degree.
elements.sent-2411-458: Second, it is possible, at least in certain cases, to upper bound the degree itself by some function of the code parameters.
elements.sent-2411-459: We show bounds of this type in Sections~\ref{subsec:decoder and \ref{subsec:randcodes .}}
elements.sent-2411-460: When such a bound is possible, it provides nontrivial bounds on code properties in terms of functions of other parameters.
elements.sent-2411-461: Encoding circuit with degree-bounded depth A particularly appealing quality of the graph formalism is that it enables us to efficiently and canonically construct encoding circuits which are bounded by a small linear function of the degree of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-462: This construction can be taken advantage of in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-463: If we already have a stabilizer code and would like to construct a nice encoding circuit, we can compile the code's tableau into a graph and then apply the procedure below.
elements.sent-2411-464: Note that Theorem~\ref{thm:encoding assumes a graph presentation, so if the ZXCF has local Cliffords, they are not included in the encoding circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-465: They can be appended to the encoding circuit produced by the theorem, at the cost of increasing the depth by 1, since all local Cliffords are single-qubit operations.
elements.sent-2411-466: Alternatively, if we have a graph which by the previously discussed procedure represents a stabilizer code, we can directly write down this encoding circuit and have a guarantee of its depth.
elements.sent-2411-467: Suppose that one may initialize qubits to the $\ket{+}$ state.
elements.sent-2411-468: Given a graph $G$ with maximum degree $\d^*$, there exists an efficient algorithm which outputs an encoding circuit $\CC$ for the code with stabilizers $\CS(G)$, such that $\operatorname{depth (\CC) \leq 2 \d^* + 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-469: We construct explicitly the transformation from the graph to its encoding circuit\footnote{A related discussion of such a transformation is given in a simultaneous work by one of the authors in \citet{khesin2024equivalence .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-470: An informal description is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-471: A graph with $|\CI| = k$ and $|\CO \cup \CP| = n$ requires a circuit taking $k$ input and $n-k$ ancillary qubits to $n$ output qubits.
elements.sent-2411-472: We choose to send the $k$ inputs to the $k$ pivots on the output sides.
elements.sent-2411-473: Edges in $G$ will generally become controlled-$Z$ gates in the encoding circuit, with the exception of edges from $\CI$ to $\CP$, which become a layer of Hadamards.
elements.sent-2411-474: More precisely, construct the circuit $\CC$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-475: Create $n$ wires, one for each node in $\CO \cup \CP$.
elements.sent-2411-476: Label the output side of the wires by the node.
elements.sent-2411-477: For each wire associated to a pivot $v$, label the input side of the wire by the input corresponding to $v$.
elements.sent-2411-478: The remaining $n-k$ wires unlabeled on the input side are the ancillary qubits.
elements.sent-2411-480: For each input $u \in \CI$ and $v \in \CO$, if $v \in o(u)$ then add a $CZ_{u, v}$ to the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-481: The order does not matter since all $CZ$ gates commute.
elements.sent-2411-483: These will correspond to the edges between each input and its corresponding pivot.
elements.sent-2411-485: There are two claims to show: (a) this construction correctly yields an encoding circuit for the code described by $\CS(G)$, and (b) $\operatorname{depth (\CC) \leq 2\d^* + 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-486: The first claim is essentially a direct consequence of the rules of the ZX calculus and the form of the ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-487: Recall that $G$ can be mapped to a ZXCF $\widetilde{G}$ by Hadamarding all edges, endowing each node with a free edge.
elements.sent-2411-488: Initially, work qubits ZX diagrams are given by $Z$ nodes, which are the $\ket{+}$ states used in (1).
elements.sent-2411-489: As we turn the ZX diagram into a circuit, the edges which connect an input node to its corresponding node on the output side of the circuit pick up the gates between them.
elements.sent-2411-490: Since we have chosen the inputs to map to their corresponding pivot on the output side of the circuit, and the only gate between them is $H$, this accounts for (3) above.
elements.sent-2411-491: The remaining edges connect distinct qubits (rather than the same qubit from the input to output side), and such edges are precisely given by $CZ$ gates.
elements.sent-2411-493: Note that the gates are ordered in this particular way because of the causal structure of the ZX diagram.
elements.sent-2411-494: First, input edges must propagate information to the output edges, so the associated input wires must have their $CZ$ gates to establish their interaction with the ancilla wires.
elements.sent-2411-495: Next, the circuit completes the interactions from input to output via the input-pivot Hadamards.
elements.sent-2411-496: Finally, the output edges interact, so the output-output $CZ$ gates are placed on the ancillary wires.
elements.sent-2411-497: This completes the proof of the first claim, correctness.
elements.sent-2411-498: To the second claim, we observe that since all $CZ$ gates commute, the depth is given by the minimum number of groupings of $CZ$ gates which act on disjoint sets of qubits.
elements.sent-2411-499: Such a problem reduces to finding the optimal edge colouring of $G$, i.e. the minimum number of colours needed to colour every edge, such that no two edges which share a node have the same colour.
elements.sent-2411-500: Although finding the optimal edge colouring is known to be NP-complete, Vizing's edge colouring theorem guarantees that the minimal colouring is at most $\d^* + 1$, and moreover there exists an efficient algorithm which finds such a colouring~\cite{vizing,misra1992constructive .}
elements.sent-2411-501: In sum, (2) and (4) each has depth at most $\d^* + 1$, and (3) has depth 1, so that the total depth is at most $2\d^* + 3$ as claimed.
elements.sent-2411-502: Although encoding is practice is often performed by a method to prepare the logical zero state, e.g. repeatedly measuring the stabilizers until they are all $+1$, Theorem~\ref{thm:encoding shows that for codes that have sufficiently small degree, it may be possible to have a much stronger guarantee, namely to efficiently encode any state directly via an encoding circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-503: Section~\ref{subsec:smallcodes gives an example of such an encoding circuit for a code we have constructed, and discusses how the depth may actually be substantially smaller in practice by solving small cases of minimal edge colouring.}
elements.sent-2411-504: Moreover, in Section~\ref{subsec:decoder , we will show that the degree, and thus the encoding circuit depth, can be bounded above by code parameters for certain families of codes.}
