elements.sent-2411-505: This result will thus directly upper bound the degree of the encoding circuit by code parameters.
elements.sent-2411-506: Logical non-Pauli gates on graph codes Earlier, we showed that the logical Pauli operations can be extracted immediately from the graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-507: Specifically, $\overline{X _v$ and $\overline{Z _v$ for $v \in \CI$ are implemented respectively by $Z(N_o(v))$ and $X(p(v)) Z(N_o(p(v)))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-508: We derived these logical Paulis by placing a Pauli on the free edge of a particular input node, and then applying ZX equivalence rules to push the Pauli through the node into the output.
elements.sent-2411-509: For a completely general logical operation $\overline{U}$ and graph code $G$, the only algorithm to physically implement $\overline{U}$ is to unencode, apply $U$, and then re-encode.
elements.sent-2411-510: Such a technique is typically undesirable, since the encoding circuit is necessarily high-depth to achieve a good distance, which implies that the logical operation's implementation strongly propagates errors and therefore resists fault tolerance.

elements.sent-2411-524: For a certain non-diagonal Clifford gate, the $\sqrt{X}$ gate, we are able to even further reduce the constant to just 1.
elements.sent-2411-525: This improvement relies on a specific ZX equivalence relation involving a purely graph transformation known as a local complementation about a vertex (LCV).
elements.sent-2411-526: The LCV is defined for any graph and has been presented under various guises in the literature~\cite{hu2022improved,backens2014zx,van2020zx .}
elements.sent-2411-527: We have, in fact, already used the LCV in Appendix~\ref{app:sec:compiler to enforce the Clifford rule of the ZXCF.}
elements.sent-2411-528: Below, we fix the vertex set $V$ and consider different graphs which can be made with the nodes in $V$.
elements.sent-2411-529: Given a graph $G = (V, E)$, let $N(u) = \set{v \in V \,:\, (u, v) \in E}$ be the neighbors of $u \in V$ in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-530: We can assume throughout this paper that $G$ does not permit self-edges; that is, $\forall u \in V,\, (u, u) \notin E$.
elements.sent-2411-531: Let $V$ be fixed and $G=(V,E)$ be a graph.
elements.sent-2411-532: A local complementation about a vertex $v\in V$ is a graph transformation $\text{LCV \,:\, \mathcal{G \times V \to \mathcal{G}$ given by $(V, E) \mapsto (V, E \,\D\, K(N(v)))$, where $K(S)$ is the edge set of a complete graph formed by nodes in $S \subseteq V$.}}
elements.sent-2411-533: Intuitively, a LC about a vertex $v$ can be described as follows.
elements.sent-2411-534: Consider the edges of the complete graph built from $N(v)$, and then toggle all these edges in $v$.
elements.sent-2411-535: That is, if $e \in E \cap K(N(v))$, then remove $e$ from $E$, and if $e \in K(N(v)) \setminus E$, add $e$ to $E$.
elements.sent-2411-536: The LCV builds a bridge between unitary operations on graph codes and inherent graph transformations.
elements.sent-2411-537: Suppose we have a graph $G = (\CI \cup \CP \cup \CO, E)$ and we wish to apply a logical unitary $\overline{U}$; that is, $\overline{U _v$ for $v \in \CI$.}

elements.sent-2411-558: Game unification of stabilizer coding algorithms Thus far, given a graph, we have described its corresponding stabilizers, given a set of logical operators, and provided a simple upper bound on the distance.
elements.sent-2411-559: To complete our fully graph formalism of stabilizer codes, we will show that important code algorithms---distance calculation or approximation, stabilizer weight reduction, and decoding---can all be expressed in a unified graph manner, namely by a single class of one-player games on the graph.

elements.sent-2411-562: Every node is endowed with both a light and a switch, both of which are binary (i.e. on of off), which are initially off.
elements.sent-2411-563: Flipping a switch at node $v$ (a) permanently destroys the light at $v$ if $v \notin \CI$ and (b) toggles all intact (i.e. non-destroyed) lights on the neighbours of $v$.

elements.sent-2411-570: The game ends when the lights reach an instance-specific desired final configuration, usually with all non-input lights off or destroyed.

elements.sent-2411-574: By correctly choosing the instance of the game, we can represent many important stabilizer coding algorithms as strategies for QLO games.

elements.sent-2411-646: Applications of the graph formalism We proceed to provide constructive evidence in three areas for the utility of a graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-647: First, in Section~\ref{subsec:smallcodes , we argue that for near-term experimental purposes, the graph formalism provides a simple and flexible prescription for the design of codes with a desired rate $R$ and distance $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-648: Intuitively, this advantage occurs because we can appeal to the geometrical and topological structure of graphs, as well as take inspiration from well-studied graphs, to get close to a distance $d$.
elements.sent-2411-649: We then describe improved results for random codes and construct an efficient graph decoder for a family of stabilizer codes constructed from graphs.
elements.sent-2411-650: Flexible code design Generally, stabilizer tableaus have proven more useful as a description rather than a constructive mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-651: Most well-known stabilizer codes constructed are actually CSS codes~\cite{panteleev2021degenerate,panteleev2022asymptotically,bravyi2024high,kitaev2003fault , and non-CSS stabilizer codes are usually found by some alternate presentation.}
elements.sent-2411-652: The lack of use of stabilizer tableaus is not unusual, as it is simply not obvious as to what collections of Pauli strings construct codes with high distance, easy decoding, gates, geometric properties etc. The universality of the graph representation of stabilizer codes, combined with a rich history of graphs in error correction~\cite{tanner1981recursive,sipser1996expander,bravyi2024high,kissinger2022phase , suggests that a promising technique to construct certain codes lies in finding sufficiently ``nice'' graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-653: Because of its universal expressive power, the graph representation in this work has parameters that scale with distance.
elements.sent-2411-654: As we showed previously, the distance is bounded above by degree parameters of the graph, and the canonical stabilizers have weights that also scale with the degree.
elements.sent-2411-655: Therefore, the graph representation is best suited for finding codes with a desired approximate numerical values of code parameters, as opposed to asymptotically good LDPC codes.
elements.sent-2411-656: In other words, graphs provide a flexible technique to generate potentially practical small-scale codes.
elements.sent-2411-657: As an example of this flexibility, if one wanted to create a code with a particular distance, one can set the degree of the graph in accordance with Corollary~\ref{corollary:degree .}
elements.sent-2411-658: On graphs that do not have large overlaps in sets of neighbours, the distance will be determined by the degree of the input vertices as well as the output-degrees of input neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-659: Achieving a certain rate translates to having a sufficiently high density of inputs, which in turn can influence the required degree of the input neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-660: Some experimental devices or algorithms may benefit from codes that have at most a particular number of physical qubits or encode a minimum number of logical qubits, both of which are easy to set in the graph formalism.
elements.sent-2411-661: If ensuring that the physical qubits behave similarly in a highly uniform manner, a graph could be chosen to be highly regular and symmetric, such as the 5-, 7-, and 9-qubit codes from Section~\ref{sec:compiler:subsec:applications .}
elements.sent-2411-662: Further still, a graph can be chosen to be planar and local in two dimensions, or perhaps supporting only a fixed number of long-range connections for ease of practical implementation.
elements.sent-2411-663: On the other hand, a graph embedded into a higher-dimensional space, such as a high-dimensional grid graph for example, could be easier to represent or simulate classically.
elements.sent-2411-664: We will give examples of codes with each of these properties below.
elements.sent-2411-665: The distance calculations of codes in this section were performed by direct computation.
elements.sent-2411-666: The code is freely available for use~\cite{Lu_graphcodes .}
elements.sent-2411-667: We now showcase a few examples of novel small codes that we constructed from the graph formalism.
elements.sent-2411-668: As a first example, we examine the 7-qubit Steane code in Fig.~\ref{fig:7-qubit-code , which takes the shape of a cube.}
elements.sent-2411-669: Such a geometric structure motivates the exploration of other highly regular geometric solids as graph codes, such as the platonic solids.
elements.sent-2411-670: While the graphs of a tetrahedron and octahedron perform rather poorly as codes due to their particular symmetries, the graph of a dodecahedron represents a relatively good code.
elements.sent-2411-671: Figure~\ref{fig:dodeca exhibits the dodecahedral code, which has parameters $[[16, 4, 3]]$.}
elements.sent-2411-672: Since the degree of every node is 3, the dodecahedral code saturates its distance-degree bound.
elements.sent-2411-673: The rate has also been optimized while not compromising the distance.
elements.sent-2411-674: To maintain a distance equal to the degree, no two inputs may share a neighbour (as otherwise there exists a logical operator of lower weight), so the maximum number of inputs that can be selected is 4.
elements.sent-2411-675: We remark that this graph is not a CSS code, being non-bipartite, but has rate $1/4$.
elements.sent-2411-676: As such, it is strictly better than perhaps the most well-known non-CSS stabilizer code, the $\llbracket5, 1, 3\rrbracket$ 5-qubit code from Section~\ref{sec:compiler:subsec:applications .}
elements.sent-2411-677: To complete our discussion of the dodecahedral code, we apply Theorem~\ref{thm:encoding to give the code a low-depth encoding circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-678: Figure~\ref{fig:dodeca-encoder illustrates two representations of the encoding circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-679: On the left, we draw a ZX diagram which appears as an intermediate step in the algorithmic construction in Theorem~\ref{thm:encoding .}
elements.sent-2411-680: On the right, we translate the ZX diagram into standard quantum circuit notation.
elements.sent-2411-681: The translation is an immediate consequence of the ZX calculus rules: a $Z$ node with a single output is a $\ket+$ state and a Hadamarded edge between two $Z$ nodes implements a $CZ$ gate.
elements.sent-2411-682: Corresponding to the theorem, the $CZ$ gates before the layer of Hadamards form the input-output edges; the Hadamards effectively form input-pivot edges; and the $CZ$ gates after the layer of Hadamards form the edges from non-inputs to non-inputs.
elements.sent-2411-683: We observe also that the application of the ZX calculus simplification rules to the diagram in Fig.~\ref{fig:dodeca-encoder , such as merging $Z$ nodes together, recovers the graph representation of the dodecahedral code itself.}
elements.sent-2411-684: In this sense the encoding circuit appears almost immediately from the graph representation.

elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Covering spaces and the 5-covered icosahedral code
elements.sent-2411-690: We next consider a code based on the graph of an icosahedron.
elements.sent-2411-691: Since the vertices have an icosahedron have degree 5, we can hope that a code based on this graph would have distance 5.
elements.sent-2411-692: Unfortunately, we find that the code underperforms the degree bound because a $Y$ gate on two antipodal vertices is equivalent to applying a $Z$ to each vertex.
elements.sent-2411-693: Hence, a logical $Y$ on any input is a logical operation of weight 3, with one $Y$ on the vertex opposite the input and two more on any two antipodal vertices.
elements.sent-2411-694: This observation also implies that if we wish to add more than one input to the icosahedron, our distance will be reduced further, as a logical operation would just consist of $Y$'s on two vertices opposite the inputs.
elements.sent-2411-695: To get around this issue, we lift the graph into a finite covering space.
elements.sent-2411-696: Specifically, we can imagine taking a double cover of the icosahedron by ``puncturing'' the icosahedron in the center of two opposite faces and attaching a winding number to each vertex, which is computed modulo 2.
elements.sent-2411-697: In doing so, we find that the double-covered graph with a single input has distance 5.
elements.sent-2411-698: In fact, we can improve the rate slightly by taking an $n$-cover of the icosahedron.
elements.sent-2411-699: Specifically, when $n=5$, we can fit 6 input vertices, where the inputs are not vertices of the punctured faces, and each of which is wound $300\degree$ around from the previous input.
elements.sent-2411-700: In Fig.~\ref{fig:icosa , if we puncture the two central faces, the inputs in the 5 copies of the icosahedron will be on copies of the 6 vertices along the perimeter, each rotated $300\degree$ from the last.}
elements.sent-2411-702: The ideas used to create the five-covered icosahedron can be extended more generally to extend otherwise finite graphs.
elements.sent-2411-703: In fact, arbitrarily complicated covers can be proposed, leading all the way up to the universal cover of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-704: If regularity and a finite set of vertices are desired, some periodic boundary conditions can be established to keep the graph finite.
elements.sent-2411-705: In general, such covering space graphs may provide a promising path towards code design recipes in this framework.
elements.sent-2411-706: In the case of Cayley graphs, similar ideas have already been utilized to construct quantum LDPC codes from Cayley graphs of free groups with generators comprised of columns of a classical parity check matrix~\cite{couvreur2013construction .}
elements.sent-2411-707: Even more generally, lattice-type codes enable the construction of families of codes with increasing degree (and thus, potentially, increasing distance) while also preserving some notions of regularity that are often desirable in code construction.
elements.sent-2411-708: If we wish to remain in two dimensions and also have a Euclidean lattice, we are restricted in the sense that the triangular lattice, which has degree 6, is the highest-degree such lattice.
elements.sent-2411-709: We numerically confirmed that such lattices do yield distance-6 codes.
elements.sent-2411-710: Beyond degree 6, we can either raise the dimension or embed graphs in non-Euclidean spaces.
elements.sent-2411-711: For example, an embedding of triangular surfaces into hyperbolic spaces yields the Poincar\'e's disk map.
elements.sent-2411-712: However, because the Poincaré disc packs infinitely many points into a finite space, we would have to construct finite approximations and then study the effect of artificially establishing a finite boundary.
elements.sent-2411-713: Analysis of such effects appears difficult and is out of the scope of this work.
elements.sent-2411-714: Nonetheless, such classes of codes in non-Euclidean spaces may prove useful, and even connected to HaPPY codes~\cite{pastawski2015holographic , which also use graphs in hyperbolic space.}
elements.sent-2411-715: The other path---increasing the dimension---is more amenable to analysis.
elements.sent-2411-716: A simple, general recipe to construct a code from a lattice is to cut off the lattice in each dimension at a finite number of intervals, endow the finite version with periodic boundary conditions in each direction, and then embed some inputs and pivots within them such that the choices of pivots are valid and inputs are not connected.
elements.sent-2411-717: One immediate advantage of this approach is a robustness to deformation that does not hold for CSS codes.
elements.sent-2411-718: In particular, we showed in Observation~\ref{obs:CSS_bipartite that a graph code is CSS if and only if it is bipartite.}
elements.sent-2411-719: For lattices with periodic boundary conditions, the bipartite condition is equivalent to the length of every single dimension of the torus being even.
elements.sent-2411-720: Such a requirement is very non-local and does not change the central architecture of the code.
elements.sent-2411-721: Even for CSS torii, the addition of one vertex far from any inputs such that it forms an odd-length cycle will make the code no longer CSS but will not meaningfully affect any of the code's parameters.
elements.sent-2411-722: When constructing families of codes in the graph formalism, it is important to consider the geometry of the graph as well as the placement of the inputs.
elements.sent-2411-723: To make use of the graph formalism for computing stabilizers and logical operations, the identification of pivot nodes is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-724: While the ZXCF does not allow pivot-pivot edges, these do not affect the expressions of the graph formalism.
elements.sent-2411-725: Thus, for easy pivot selection, it suffices for all of the inputs to be separated by paths of length at least 3 from each other in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-726: Although such a restriction is not necessary for code construction, it does allow for any input's neighbour to be chosen as its pivot as that node would necessarily not border any other inputs by the distance condition.
elements.sent-2411-727: The downside to such graphs, however is that their rate if bounded as $R \leq \frac{1 {d}$, where $d$ is the degree of the graph (assuming it is regular).}
elements.sent-2411-728: In other words, they have a strong rate-distance tradeoff.
elements.sent-2411-729: Nonetheless, such graphs may be interesting at small scales, as we have already seen.
elements.sent-2411-730: In higher dimensions, perhaps the simplest lattice we can utilize to build a code is the boolean hypercube in an arbitrary $m$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-731: We represent the hypercube by labeling nodes as length-$m$ bitstrings and connecting two nodes if their Hamming distance is exactly 1, i.e. they differ by one bit flip.
elements.sent-2411-732: We observe immediately that the hypercube code is CSS for all $m$, since the hypercube is bipartite.
elements.sent-2411-733: To select inputs, we observe that if we wish to place inputs such that they are separated by paths of length at least 3, we can leverage classical coding theory to provide this guarantee automatically.
elements.sent-2411-734: Specifically, we consider the classical $[2^r - 1, 2^r - r - 1, 3]$ Hamming codes~\cite{hamming1950error .}
elements.sent-2411-735: Letting $m = 2^r - 1$, we can equivalently express the parameters as $[m, m - \log(m+1), 3]$, where the code is perfect when $m$ is one less than a power of 2.
elements.sent-2411-736: Note that all logarithms in this paper are taken to be base 2.
elements.sent-2411-737: Since this code has $m - \log(m+1)$ logical bits, there are a total of $\frac{2^m {m + 1}$ codewords represented as length-$m$ bitstrings.}
elements.sent-2411-738: Because the Hamming code's distance is 3, all such codewords are guaranteed to have a separation in the hypercube by paths of length at least 3.
elements.sent-2411-739: Therefore, by choosing the input nodes in the $m$-dimensional hypercube to be those whose bitstring is a codeword in the $[m, m - \log(m+1), 3]$ Hamming code, we obtain a code with $\frac{2^m {m+1}$ logical qubits, $2^m -\frac{2^m {m+1 = \frac{m 2^m {m+1}$ physical qubits, and a distance $D_m$ bounded above by the degree $m$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-740: That is, a $\llbracket\frac{m 2^m {m+1 , \frac{2^m {m+1 , D_m\rrbracket$ family of codes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-741: Due to the length-3 separation of inputs, any choice of pivots uniquely connected to a corresponding input will do; later, we will show that it is both possible and particularly convenient to choose the pivots such that they are also separated by paths of length at least 3.
elements.sent-2411-742: We will also later prove in Section~\ref{subsec:decoder that $D_m \gtrsim m/2$, up to an additive error of 2, and give evidence that $D_m = m$.}

elements.sent-2411-746: We conclude this section with some remarks on how such codes may be constructed.
elements.sent-2411-747: In a grid lattice on a $m$-dimensional torus, we wish to pack inputs more closely while being mindful of the fact that our distance will be bounded above by the number of output nodes next to each output node.
elements.sent-2411-748: One such construction is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-749: First, consider a foliation of the $m$-torus into sequentially numbered ``layers'' of $(m-1)$-dimensional toric hypersurfaces that together stack into a $m$-dimensional torus.
elements.sent-2411-750: In each layer, we will consider sets of vertices grouped by their index, the sum of their integer coordinates taken mod 3.
elements.sent-2411-751: We can assume that the size of each torus dimension is a multiple of 3 to make sure these sets are well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-752: Note that the index does not consider the $m$th coordinate, as that is determined by the number of the layer that a node ends up in.
elements.sent-2411-753: In layer 1, we set all nodes of index 0 to be inputs, with pivots in layer 2.
elements.sent-2411-754: In layer 2, we set all nodes of index 0 or 1 to be pivots.
elements.sent-2411-755: In layer 3, we set all nodes of index 1 to be inputs, with pivots in layer 2.
elements.sent-2411-756: We repeat this 3-layer structure as many times as desired.
elements.sent-2411-757: Such a pattern is essentially a 3-layered, high-dimensional analog of a checkerboard pattern.
elements.sent-2411-758: Again, we see that our distance upper bound is $d$, as vertices such as those of index 1 in layer 1 have $d$ inputs among their $2d$ neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-759: More sophisticated techniques are necessary, however, to lower-bound the distance.
elements.sent-2411-760: However, the rate of this code is a constant $\frac27$, which is achieved at any scale of this code, without the need for extreme numbers of qubits for the asymptotic behaviour of the code parameters to be expressed.
elements.sent-2411-761: Additionally, we note that if an odd number of 3-layer structures are used or if any of the dimensions of each layer is odd, the code graph will have a cycle of odd length, meaning that the resulting code will not be a CSS code.
elements.sent-2411-762: These examples showcase a wide variety of families and constructions, both small and large, that can be achieved with the graph formalism.
elements.sent-2411-763: While some are sufficiently small to admit brute-force computational distance calculations, others are more difficult to analyze and will require additional distance-bounding tools to understand.
elements.sent-2411-764: We take a first step in this direction in the next section.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H]\caption{Greedy graph decoder} \label{alg:greedy} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{FMain}{GreedyDecoder} \SetKwProg{Fn}{Function}{:}{} \Fn{\FMain{$G$}}{ $\text{explored} \leftarrow [],\, (v_0, n_0) \leftarrow (\text{nil}, 0)$\; \While(// X error recovery loop){True}{ \For{$v \in \CO \cup \CP$}{ $\text{gap} \leftarrow 2 \text{NumLights}(o(v)) - |o(v)|$\; \tcp{If more than half of lights around $v$ are on, `gap' is positive} \If{$\text{gap} > n_0$}{ $v_0 = v,\, n_0 = \text{gap}$\; } } \If{$v_0 \in explored$ or $\text{gap} = 0$}{ break\; } Recover$_X(v_0)$ \tcp{toggle lights at $o(v_0) \,\D\, oip(v_0)$, destroy light at $v_0$} Append $v_0$ to explored\; } $\text{explored} \leftarrow [],\, (v_0, n_0) \leftarrow (\text{nil}, 0)$ \tcp{Reset tracking variables} \While(// Z error on pivots recovery loop){True}{ \For{$v \in \CP$}{ $\text{gap} \leftarrow 2 \text{NumLights}(oi(v)) - |oi(v)|$\; \If{$\text{gap} > n_0$}{ $v_0 = v,\, n_0 = \text{gap}$\; } } \If{$v_0 \in explored$ or $\text{gap} = 0$}{ break\; } Recover$_Z(v_0)$ \tcp{toggle lights at $oi(v_0)$} Append $v_0$ to explored\; } \For(// Z error on outputs recovery loop){$v \in \CO$}{ \If{LightIsOn$(v)$}{ Recover$_Z(v)$ \tcp{Destroy light at $v$} } } \KwRet }
elements.sent-2411-779: The greedy decoder is a strategy in the decoding instance of QLO.
elements.sent-2411-780: In particular, the operations permitted are Recover$_X$ and Recover$_Z$, which flip switches or destroy lights depending on the node $v$ chosen.
elements.sent-2411-781: We showed earlier in Theorem~\ref{thm:decodingQLO how a QLO strategy maps to a physical recovery operation.}
elements.sent-2411-782: The construction of the algorithm is based on the following intuition.
elements.sent-2411-783: If $v \in \CO$ suffers a $X$ error, the stabilizers at $o(v) \,\D\, oip(v)$ will measure $-1$, so in the QLO game the lights in $o(v) \,\D\, oip(v)$ will turn on.
elements.sent-2411-784: Perhaps there will be some $Z$ errors in $o(v) \subseteq \CO$, each of which correspond to turning on a single light in $o(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-785: Therefore, assuming it is difficult to turn on more than half of the lights in $o(v)$, a good guess is that if more than half of the lights are on in $o(v)$ then $v$ has suffered an $X$ error, at which point we can apply $X$ on $v$ to reverse the error, via Recover$_X$.
elements.sent-2411-786: Note that we do not consider the status of the lights in $oip(v)$ when deciding whether to toggle $v$, as all lights in $oip(v)$ can be toggled by applying an $X$ to any neighbour of $p(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-787: The recovery may itself turn on more lights in other places, so to avoid issues related to parallelism, we work in sequence and apply $X$ recoveries one at a time, in order of nodes which have the most neighbours with lights on.
elements.sent-2411-788: A similar story holds for $Z$ errors on $v \in \CP$.
elements.sent-2411-789: They are detected by stabilizers in $oi(v)$, so we apply the analogous greedy recovery operation there.
elements.sent-2411-790: Finally, for remaining lights on in $v \in \CO$, we directly destroy the lights with $Z$ operations.
elements.sent-2411-791: The key property within this intuition that seems to enable greedy-type decoding is the idea that it must be difficult to ``fake'' an $X$ error on some $v$ by using a small amount of moves to turn on most of the lights in $o(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-792: We now formalize this property to give a simple performance guarantee of the greedy decoder.
elements.sent-2411-793: In this definition, we are specifically interested in how many lights an operation at node $v$ can toggle in the set $o(u)$, the set we examine to determine whether to toggle the switch at $u$.
elements.sent-2411-794: Let $G = (V, \set{o, i, p )$ be a graph with input, output, and valid pivot nodes.}

elements.sent-2411-890: Random codes with reduced stabilizer weights One direct technique for which a graph formalism enables more controlled analysis is random codes.
elements.sent-2411-891: Under the stabilizer tableau representation, the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov-bound gives an asymptotic (large $n$) relation between code parameters $n, k, d$ that, if satisfied, guarantee the existence of a $\llbracket n, k, d\rrbracket$ code.

elements.sent-2411-893: The proof proceeds probabilistically, namely by generating a uniformly random stabilizer tableau and arguing that such a tableau has a nonzero probability of having distance $d$ if the bound is satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-894: However, because the expected weight (i.e. number of non-identity elements) of a random Pauli string $P_{1 \otimes \cdots \otimes P_{n}$, where $P_i \in \set{I, X, Y, Z}$, is $O(n)$, the random codes generated in the Gilbert-Varshamov fashion will have linear stabilizer weight.}
elements.sent-2411-895: A natural question is whether the average weight can be decreased by a stochastic algorithm that is more fine-grained than uniform randomness.
elements.sent-2411-896: Such a task has proven difficult in the stabilizer tableau representation due to the challenge of generating Paulis that both commute and are not uniformly distributed.
elements.sent-2411-897: On the other hand, we can easily generate random graphs of a certain structure by defining first the possible edges that may be included, and then randomly choosing edges to include in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-898: This insight enables us to extend the result of the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov bound by way of the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-899: Let $n$ be the number of physical qubits and define $R(n) < 1$ and $d(n) \in [\w(1), \frac{n {\log n ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-900: There exists an efficiently sampleable family of distributions $\CS_n$, parameterized by $n$, over stabilizer codes that as $n \to \infty$ satisfy three properties.

elements.sent-2411-947: Conclusion \& Outlook We introduced a graph structure which universally represents all stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-948: Stabilizer tableaus can be efficiently compiled into such graphs, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-949: Our primary motivation for such a representation was to gain access to natural graph properties and notions---degree, geometry, connectivity---and then leverage them to improve code construction and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-950: As first steps in this direction, we chose several geometric shapes discretized into graphs and analyzed them as code representations.
elements.sent-2411-951: In doing so, we found a number of constant-size codes with desirable rates and reasonably large numerical distances, as well as a family of hypercube codes that have near-linear rate, logarithmic distance, and good encoding/decoding properties.
elements.sent-2411-952: In similar spirit, we also constructed a class of codes for which, given a desired rate $R$ and distance $d$, a random code drawn from the class has high probability of having rate $R$, distance $d$, and, so long as $R$ and $d$ were not both too large, nontrivially bounded stabilizer weights.
elements.sent-2411-953: This analysis extends the result of the quantum Gilbert-Varshamov bound, which has a distance-rate tradeoff but no bound on stabilizer weight due to its coarse-grained analysis.
elements.sent-2411-954: More generally, we showed that graph properties are intimately tied to code properties.
elements.sent-2411-955: Key code properties (weight, distance, encoding and logical operator circuit depths) are all controlled by small linear functions of graph degrees, and key coding algorithms (distance approximation, weight reduction, decoding) are all strategies on various instances of quantum lights out (QLO) games.
elements.sent-2411-956: The former observation allowed us to leverage graph algorithms to efficiently construct encoding circuits with controlled depth upper bounds as well as build logical operation circuits whose depths, in the case of diagonal or certain Clifford gates, are conceptually distinct and quantitatively better than simply unencoding, applying, and re-encoding.
elements.sent-2411-957: The latter result led us to design an efficient greedy QLO strategy for decoding, and prove that it succeeds up to a small constant factor on at least a certain class of graphs which include the hypercube code.
elements.sent-2411-958: We believe that further study in graph-based decoding algorithms will reveal efficient decoders for many other families of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-959: In sum, the universal graph representation enables insights and improvements that are general across all or large classes of stabilizer codes, by providing avenues of construction and analysis that are substantially less obvious via conventional representations of additive quantum codes.
elements.sent-2411-960: There are several directions in which to explore from this point.
elements.sent-2411-962: Classically, graph representations have shown a strong correspondence between graph properties and code properties beyond simple objects like the degree.
elements.sent-2411-963: Notably, spectral expander graphs have led to good classical codes.
elements.sent-2411-964: A characterization effort connecting graph properties such as spectrum, expansion, cycle sizes, density, geometry, and topology to distance, decoding, stabilizer weight, etc. could significantly extend this work's insight into stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-965: It is also worthwhile to more deeply study the quantum lights out game, as insights onto their strategies have equivalent interpretations on coding algorithm performance.
elements.sent-2411-966: First steps towards the worst- and average-case analysis of coding algorithms based on the hardness of certain computational problems have appeared recently~\cite{poremba2024learning , and their connection to QLO is an interesting question.}
elements.sent-2411-967: A second direction is directly constructive.
elements.sent-2411-968: Given particular architectures or algorithms of experimental interest, is it possible to systematically design graphs whose codes are particularly suitable for such devices or algorithms in terms of locality of connectivity, geometry, rate, and distance? The third involves refining the approach of this work.
elements.sent-2411-969: Representations that are universal are doomed in certain aspects of coding because their generality implies that properties scale with distance.
elements.sent-2411-970: If there exists a collection of graphs that, in exchange for representing a restricted subset of stabilizer codes, satisfies low-weight stabilizers, it may open new paths forward for practical code constructions and corresponding algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-971: Such a representation would essentially lie in an optimal middle ground between the approach of surface-type code constructions and the universal graph representation.

elements.sent-2411-977: In the main text, we sketched briefly the algorithm that maps encoders to ZXCFs.
elements.sent-2411-978: Here, we give a detailed explication.
elements.sent-2411-979: The first part of our algorithm maps the encoder into some ZX diagram; we can then transform it via a sequence of equiavlence rules into a ZXCF.
elements.sent-2411-980: To accomplish a transformation into ZX diagrams, we first observe prior work by \citet{hu2022improved for stabilizer states.}
elements.sent-2411-981: Although Hu and Khesin did not use ZX calculus in their work, their construction uses graph states decorated with single-qubit operators, which can be mapped directly to ZX calculus by a manner we will briefly describe.
elements.sent-2411-982: A HK diagram is constructed by starting with a graph state---a state corresponding to an undirected graph wherein the nodes become $\ket{+}$ qubits and the edges become controlled-$Z$ gates.
elements.sent-2411-983: Each node is then endowed with a single free edge, which does not connect to any other node.
elements.sent-2411-984: Local Clifford operations in $\langle H,S,Z\rangle$ are then applied to the qubits.
elements.sent-2411-985: Such a construction has the capacity to express any stabilizer state.
elements.sent-2411-986: A direct mapping enables the presentation of a HK diagram into the ZX calculus.
elements.sent-2411-987: HK vertices become $Z$ nodes, as a vertex in a graph state begins in the state $\ket+$, which is a $Z$ node with a single output.
elements.sent-2411-988: HK (non-free) edges become (non-free) edges with Hadamard gates, as both of these correspond to $CZ$ gates.
elements.sent-2411-989: HK local operations of $S$, $Z$, or $SZ$ become phases on the corresponding node of $\frac\pi2$, $\pi$, and $\frac{3\pi 2$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-990: The local operation $H$ becomes a Hadamard gate on the node's free edge.
elements.sent-2411-991: Thus, we will without loss consider HK forms to be in the ZX calculus representation and denote them ZX-HK forms.
elements.sent-2411-992: We remark that transformations of similar decorated graph state families into ZX calculus presentations have been considered in other contexts, such as in \citet{backens2014zx .}
elements.sent-2411-993: The work of \citet{hu2022improved is limited to stabilizer states.}
elements.sent-2411-994: However, by vector space duality one can equivalently transform operators into states and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-995: There exists an efficient transformation of a ZX encoder diagram with $n-k$ input edges and $n$ output edges into a corresponding ZX presentation of a stabilizer state in ZX-HK form, with $n + (n-k) = 2n-k$ free edges.
elements.sent-2411-996: We will turn the encoder diagram into a Clifford circuit, and then map the output of that circuit (with input the all-$\ket{+}$ state) to HK form.
elements.sent-2411-997: Any $X$ (red) nodes in the ZX encoder diagram can be transformed into $Z$ (green) nodes with the same phase surrounded by Hadamard gates.
elements.sent-2411-998: Now, we can interpret the ZX encoder diagram as a state by treating the input edges as output edges (the ZX version of the Choi-Jamio\l{ kowski isomorphism).}
elements.sent-2411-999: Our ZX diagram now computes a state, so we can express it entirely in terms of the following elementary operations and their circuit analogs.
elements.sent-2411-1000: First, we could have a $Z$ node with only one output or only one input.
elements.sent-2411-1001: In a circuit, this is a $\ket+$ qubit or a post-selected $\bra+$ measurement, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1002: We can also have a Hadamarded edge or a node with $\frac\pi2$ phase, which are represented as $H$ or $S$ gates in circuits, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1003: Lastly, we can have a $Z$ node of degree 3, with either 2 inputs and 1 output or 1 input and 2 outputs that acts as a merge or a split.
elements.sent-2411-1004: This is equivalent to applying a $CX$ operation between two qubits where the second qubit is either initialized to $\ket0$ before the $CX$ gate or post-selected by the measurement $\bra0$ after the $CX$ gate, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Any ZX diagram can be expressed in terms of these operations \cite{backens2014zx .}
elements.sent-2411-1006: Furthermore, as we apply each of these operations we can keep track of the current HK form for our state.
elements.sent-2411-1007: Application of Lemma \ref{lemma:ZXencoder_to_HK results in a ZX-HK form.}
elements.sent-2411-1008: That is, there is one $Z$ node per vertex of the graph in HK form, with any internal edge having a Hadamard gate on it.
elements.sent-2411-1009: Free edges can only have Hadamard gates on them if their phase is a multiple of $\pi$, corresponding to the local Clifford gates $H$ and $HZ$ in HK form, and if the associated node is not connected to any lower-numbered nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1010: Nodes whose free edges have no Hadamard gate are free to have any multiple of $\frac\pi2$ as a phase, corresponding to local Clifford operations $I$, $S$, $Z$, or $SZ$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1011: We are now equipped with a ZX-HK diagram that represents a state.
elements.sent-2411-1012: This diagram has only output edges.
elements.sent-2411-1013: To return it into an encoder diagram, we partition the $2n-k$ free edges into $n-k$ input edges and $n$ output edges.
elements.sent-2411-1014: In the circuit representation, this is equivalent to turning bra's into ket's and vice versa to map between an operator and a state.
elements.sent-2411-1016: Now, if there are any edges between input nodes (those in $\CI$), we can simply remove them.
elements.sent-2411-1017: They correspond to controlled-$Z$ operations, and we can take off any unitary operation on the input by the encoder definition.
elements.sent-2411-1018: The same goes for local Cliffords on $\CI$.
elements.sent-2411-1019: At this stage, the obtained ZX-HK form is in encoder-respecting form.
elements.sent-2411-1020: It also satisfies the Edge and Hadamard rules---there are no input edges in ZX-HK form so the analogous rule is enforced only on lowered-numbered nodes, but if we number the nodes from the beginning such that the input nodes are lowered-numbered than all output nodes, then the transformed ZX-HK form will satisfy our ZXCF Hadamard rule.
elements.sent-2411-1021: So, all that remains is to simplify the diagram to obey the RREF and Clifford rules.
elements.sent-2411-1022: An encoder diagram in ZX-HK form can be efficiently transformed to satisfy the RREF rule, while continuing to satisfy the Edge and Hadamard rules.
elements.sent-2411-1023: This proof relies on two basic properties of ZX calculus.
elements.sent-2411-1024: The first is that a $CX$ gate can be expressed on a pair of qubits by applying a pair of spiders connected by an edge, with a $Z$ spider on the control and an $X$ spider on the target.
elements.sent-2411-1025: The second fact is a rewrite rule called the bialgebra rule \cite{backens2014zx , which allows us to rewrite a green node connected to a red node by the following sequence of steps.}
elements.sent-2411-1026: First, remove the two spiders and the edge between them.
elements.sent-2411-1027: Next, add a red node on each edge previously leading into the green node; do the same for the red node by adding green nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1028: Lastly, connect each red node to each green node.
elements.sent-2411-1029: Applying a $CX$ gate to a pair of inputs is a unitary operation and does not change our code.
elements.sent-2411-1030: The control spider of the $CX$ gate fuses with the first input.
elements.sent-2411-1031: The target spider is used to perform the bialgebra rule with the second input.
elements.sent-2411-1032: This results in two green nodes where the $CX$ target used to be.
elements.sent-2411-1033: The first is merged with the first input while the second takes the place of the recently-destroyed second input.
elements.sent-2411-1034: Meanwhile, the red nodes on each input-output edge previously connected to the second input now find themselves connected to both the first and second input, and turn green after commuting through the Hadamard gate on the input-output edge, merging with the output on the other side.
elements.sent-2411-1035: Any doubled edges are removed by the Hopf rule \cite{backens2014zx .}
elements.sent-2411-1036: The result is that we added the row of $M_{\mathcal{D }$ corresponding to the first input the the row of the second.}
elements.sent-2411-1037: This allows to do arbitrary row operations since we are working in a field of characteristic 2.
elements.sent-2411-1038: This concludes the proof of the fact that $M_{\mathcal{D }$ can be turned into RREF without affecting the Edge and Hadamard rules.}
elements.sent-2411-1039: The only remaining task is to enforce the Clifford rule.
elements.sent-2411-1040: For any pivot node $p \in \CP$ with a non-zero phase, denote $v_{\text{in }$ its associated input node.}
elements.sent-2411-1041: We apply a local complementation about $v_{\text{in }$, which notably does not change the entries of $M_\mathcal{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1042: However, this operation also increases the phase of each neighbours of $v_{\text{in }$ by $\frac\pi2$ (due to multiplication by $S$) so we repeat this process until the phase of that pivot vanishes.}
elements.sent-2411-1043: The local complementation transformation is a ZX equivalence identity, and therefore is a valid operation on our diagram~\cite{backens2014zx .}
elements.sent-2411-1044: Lastly, if there are any edges between pivots $p_1$ and $p_2$, we can remove them by applying a different transformation $\phi$ to their pair of associated input nodes $v_1$ and $v_2$.
elements.sent-2411-1045: Let $N_1$ be the set of the neighbours of $v_1$ as well as $v_1$ itself and let $N_2$ be defined respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1046: The effect of $\phi$ is to toggle all edges in the set $N_1\times N_2$, including multiplicity.
elements.sent-2411-1047: This means that edges that get toggled twice are not affected.
elements.sent-2411-1048: Any self-edge of the form $(v,v)$ is treated as a $Z$ operation on vertex $v$.
elements.sent-2411-1049: In addition, $\phi$ also applies an additional local operation of $HZ$ to each of $v_1$ and $v_2$, but this can be removed as $v_1$ and $v_2$ are input vertices.
elements.sent-2411-1050: As a result of this, we have swapped the neighbours of $v_1$ and $v_2$ as well as toggled the one pivot-pivot edge we wish to flip, $(p_1,p_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-1051: Note that $\phi$ does not violate the RREF rule because once this process is complete we can simply swap the neighbours back (without any toggling) via a row operation.
elements.sent-2411-1052: This also does not add any local operations to the nodes $p_1$ and $p_2$, as each is only in one of $N_1$ and $N_2$, and thus does not receive a $Z$ operation.
elements.sent-2411-1053: We repeat until no pivot-pivot edges remain.
elements.sent-2411-1054: With that step, the transformation from Clifford encoder to ZXCF is complete.
elements.sent-2411-1055: Although this process may seem lengthy, all of these steps can be done systematically in an efficient manner, without having to go back to fix earlier rules.
elements.sent-2411-1057: Creating the HK diagram for the encoder requires us to apply $O(n)$ Pauli projections, each of which can be applied in $O(n^2)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-1058: After the HK form is created, operations such as row-reducing a matrix also take $O(n^3)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-1059: We suspect that the time complexity cannot be improved in the worst case, but that there is a lot of room for heuristic runtime improvements and optimizations.
elements.section-2411-009: Derivation of the ZXCF counting recursion
elements.sent-2411-1060: We conclude this section with the derivation of the recursive counting formula of ZXCF diagrams, given in Eq.~(\ref{eq:zxcf-count ).}
elements.sent-2411-1061: To derive $f$, imagine that, starting with two empty bins, we must assign the $n-k$ output nodes to be pivots (case $A$) or non-pivots (case $B$), where pivots need to be matched with input nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1062: The current number of pivots is tracked by $p$ and the number of non-pivot outputs is tracked by $o$.
elements.sent-2411-1063: Suppose we want the next output node to be a pivot (in $\CP$).
elements.sent-2411-1064: Since there is a one-to-one correspondence between pivots and inputs, we can add pivots only if $n > k$.
elements.sent-2411-1065: The matching between pivots and input nodes is fully constrained by the RREF rule, which sorts the inputs and pivots together.
elements.sent-2411-1066: The Clifford rule says that no pivot nodes may have local Clifford operations, so we just need to choose the edges connecting them to nodes we have already assigned.
elements.sent-2411-1067: Since there are no pivot-pivot edges and the pivot connects to only one input, we have exactly $2^o$ possibilities.
elements.sent-2411-1068: Having made an assignment, $p$ increases by 1, and $n$ decreases by 1 (both input and output are decremented since the pivot matches with an input).
elements.sent-2411-1069: Suppose instead we want the next output node to be a non-pivot (in $\CO$).
elements.sent-2411-1070: Then we have to choose the local Clifford operation as well as its edges to previously assigned vertices.
elements.sent-2411-1071: If we choose to connect the node to none of the $p$ assigned inputs, $p$ assigned pivots, or $o$ assigned vertices, then we are allowed to place any of the 6 local Cliffords on the node.
elements.sent-2411-1072: If any of those edges are present, however, we cannot apply a Hadamard to the output edge due to the Hadamard rule.
elements.sent-2411-1073: Hence, 4 choices remain for the local Clifford operation.
elements.sent-2411-1074: This works out to a total of $4(2^{2p+o -1)+6=2^{2p+o+2 +2$ possibilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-1075: To finish, we decrement the number of output qubits without changing the number of inputs.
elements.sent-2411-1076: The quantum Gilbert-Varshamov bound, Theorem~\ref{thm:quantum_gv , is discussed in, e.g., \citet{nielsen2002quantum .}}
elements.sent-2411-1077: For completeness, we provide a self-contained proof.
elements.sent-2411-1078: We proceed via a probabilistic method.
elements.sent-2411-1079: That is, we compute the probability that a random code has parameters $[[n, k, d]]$ with $n$ large.
elements.sent-2411-1080: To construct a random stabilizer code, we begin with $Z_1, \dots, Z_{n-k}$ as stabilizers, and then choose a random Clifford operator $U$ on $n$ qubits.
elements.sent-2411-1081: The random stabilizer code is given by $S_1 := U Z_1 U^\dag, \dots S_{n-l := U Z_{n-k U^\dag$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1082: Such a code is a uniformly random stabilizer code because (a) all generators commute and (b) a random Clifford takes distinct non-Identity Paulis to uniformly random non-Identity Paulis.
elements.sent-2411-1083: Fix a $n$-qubit non-Identity Pauli $P$ and note that $P' := U P U^\dag$ is a fixed uniformly random non-Identity Pauli.
elements.sent-2411-1084: Let $p = \Pr[[P, S_1] = 0, \dots, [P, S_{n-k ] = 0] = \Pr[[P', Z_1] = 0, \dots, [P', Z_{n-k ] = 0]$ be the probability that $P$ commutes with the stabilizer tableau.}}
elements.sent-2411-1085: If $P'$ commutes with $Z_i$, then the Pauli on the $i$th qubit must be either $I$ or $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-1086: There are $2^{n-k 4^k - 1$ non-identity Paulis which have $I$ or $Z$ on the first $n-k$ qubits, and $4^{n - 1$ total non-identity Paulis.}}
elements.sent-2411-1088: On the other hand, the total number of non-identity Paulis with weight at most $d$ is \begin{equation}N = \sum_{j = 1}^{d} 3^j \binom{n}{j} \leq d 3^d \binom{n}{d} ,\end{equation} using the assumption that $d \leq \frac{n {2}$ so that the last term is the greatest.}

elements.sent-2411-1230: As advancements in the experimental scaling of quantum computers have steadily marched forward, the problem of designing good quantum codes for the practical implementation of various quantum algorithms has in turn become of more pressing interest~\cite{e1,e2,e3,e4,e5 .}
elements.sent-2411-1231: Roughly speaking, stabilizer codes are the most general quantum analogue of linear codes for classical computing.
elements.sent-2411-1232: More precisely, a $\llbracket n, k\rrbracket$ stabilizer code encodes $k$ logical qubits to $n$ physical qubits by embedding $2^k$ logical degrees of freedom into a subspace defined as the simultaneous $+1$ eigenspace of $n-k$ independent commuting $n$-qubit Pauli operators, known as the stabilizers.
elements.sent-2411-1233: For this reason, much of the constructive effort in quantum coding theory has relied on developing sophisticated CSS-type product operations that generate CSS codes from nice classical codes~\cite{tillich2013quantum,panteleev2021almost .}

elements.sent-2411-1235: Classical codes are described by a $(n-k) \times n$ binary matrix $H$ whose kernel over $\mathbb{F _2$ defines the code space.}
elements.sent-2411-1236: Just as it is not obvious which tables of Paulis constitute good stabilizer codes, so too is it a priori unclear which tables of bits correspond to classical codes.
elements.sent-2411-1237: Consequently, graph representations such as Tanner graphs, which encode $H$ into a bipartite graph, were developed to give insight into classical code construction.
elements.sent-2411-1238: Tanner graphs built the bridge between expander graphs and classical codes and significantly contributed to modern classical coding through the utilization of graph theory insights~\cite{sipser1996expander .}
elements.sent-2411-1239: Even in the quantum realm, graph reasoning has already enabled breakthroughs in constructions of CSS codes.
elements.sent-2411-1240: Most famously, the topological structure of Euclidean lattices with periodic boundary conditions motivated the development of the toric and other surface codes~\cite{kitaev2003fault,bravyi2024high .}
elements.sent-2411-1241: The critical contributions of graphs in both classical and quantum coding in many specific constructive cases naturally lead to the question of whether one can use graph theory to study additive quantum codes in the most general sense.
elements.sent-2411-1242: That is, can we devise graph techniques to reason about quantum stabilizer codes in an inherently general quantum manner, without having to restrict ourselves to CSS codes and thereby reduce the problem to classical codes? elements.figure-2411-032: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{QLO_Decoding.jpg} \caption{Illustration of decoding a stabilizer code as a quantum lights out (QLO) game. For shorthand, we let $oip(v) := o(i(p(v)))$ and $oi(v) := o(i(v))$, where $o(v), i(v)$, and $p(v)$ are as in Definition~\ref{def:neighbour_sets}. In (a), errors occur on physical bits, depicted by some Paulis on output nodes. Here, the lights are toggled not by the ZX-graph rule from Lemma~\ref{lemma:rules}, but by which nodes whose corresponding stabilizer anticommutes with the error (i.e. stabilizers which measure to a violated syndrome). For $X$ on an output node $v \in \CO \cup \CP$, as shown in (b), the affected stabilizers are those corresponding to $o(v)$ the non-pivot output neighbours and $oip(v)$ the non-pivot output neighbours of the inputs corresponding to the pivot neighbours of $v$; their lights thus turn on. Generally, if a node is in $o(v) \cap oip(v)$, it will be toggled twice and not turn on; however, in the depicted graph $o(v)$ and $oip(v)$ are disjoint. elements.sent-2411-1432: Any decoding procedure can be split into steps, where each step involves applying a single-qubit Pauli to an output node.

elements.sent-2411-1439: Applying an $X$ to a node $v \in \CO \cup \CP$ therefore toggles all lights in $o(v) \,\D\, o(i(p(v)))$.
elements.sent-2411-1440: The symmetric difference is present because syndromes corresponding to nodes in $o(v) \cap o(i(p(v)))$ are flipped twice and thus identically not flipped at all.
elements.sent-2411-1441: We wish to reframe the action of Paulis on the syndromes in such a way that we can identify all Paulis operators as a choice of either flipping a switch, which toggles lights on neighbours, or toggling a light.
elements.sent-2411-1442: If we apply $Z$ on $v \in \CP$, we toggle syndromes $oi(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-1443: One way to achieve this by using switches and toggles is to flip the switch on $i(v) \in \CI$ and then toggle $v$.
elements.sent-2411-1444: By construction, flipping the switch on $i(v)$ toggles lights in the neighbours $N(i(v))$, and $N(i(v)) = \set{v \cup o(i(v))$ since $i(v)$ is only connected to a single pivot, which is by definition $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-1445: Hence, if we flip the switch on $i(v)$ and then toggle $v$, we equivalently toggle $oi(v)$ as desired.
elements.sent-2411-1446: But flipping switches on nodes in $\CI$ do not cost moves and can be absorbed into the arbitrary allowed action in round (1).
elements.sent-2411-1447: Hence, the application of $Z$ on $v \in \CP$ is equivalent to some costless moves in round (1) plus a single toggle of the light in $v$ in round (2).
elements.sent-2411-1448: Similarly, when applying $X$ to $v \in \CO \cup \CP$, we flip the syndromes in $o(v) \,\Delta\, o(i(p(v)))$ and therefore want to toggle exactly those lights using switches and toggles.
elements.sent-2411-1449: We may do so by flipping the switch at $v$ as well as all switches in $i(p(v))$; this toggles $o(v) \,\Delta\, o(i(p(v)))$ as desired, and costs only a single move because the switches flipped in $i(p(v))$ are costless and absorbed into round (1).
elements.sent-2411-1450: Conversely, any switch flip or light toggle can be associated with the construction of a recovery operator.
elements.sent-2411-1451: Specifically, a switch flip on $\CO \cup \CP$ is associated with a $X$ operator, and a light toggle on $\CO \cup \CP$ is associated with a $Z$ operator.
elements.sent-2411-1452: This association differs from the true action only by switch flips on input nodes, which are costless logical operations that do not affect the syndrome and can be absorbed into round (1).
elements.sent-2411-1453: Finally, when all lights are off, by our construction, all the syndromes are zero, so the decoding process has finished.
elements.sent-2411-1454: We note that destroying a light at a node after applying the switch at that node represents ambivalence between applying an $X$ or a $Y$, but if Alice is interested in finding the proper recovery map instead of just the map's size, she should leave all lights unbroken.

elements.sent-2411-1457: The affected stabilizers (those giving a violated syndrome) have their lights turned on according to a specific depicted pattern, and the sequence of moves to turn the lights off corresponds to the recovery operation.

elements.sent-2411-1461: It is illustrative to compare the decoding QLO game to a classical analog.
elements.sent-2411-1462: For a classical code with a Tanner graph $G = (L, R, E)$, where $L$ and $R$ are respectively the left and right node sets and $E$ is the edge set, we may associate every check node in $R$ with a light and every data node in $L$ with a switch, such that flipping the switch toggles the neighbouring lights.
elements.sent-2411-1463: At the start of decoding, a light is on if the check corresponding to that node is violated.
elements.sent-2411-1464: We then iteratively choose data bits to flip and update the syndromes.
elements.sent-2411-1465: This is precisely equivalent to flipping switches on the data nodes, which toggle the check node lights.
elements.sent-2411-1466: When all lights are off, the recovered data must be a codeword.
elements.sent-2411-1467: This classical version of lights out on Tanner graphs is thus NP-complete as well.
elements.sent-2411-1468: At the same time, classical lights out can be solved efficiently if the graph is a sufficiently good expander, using a simple greedy algorithm~\cite{sipser1996expander .}
elements.sent-2411-1469: We later show that if a graph of a stabilizer code satisfies a different but related structural property, then a greedy QLO strategy can also efficiently decode errors.

elements.sent-2411-1472: For instance, we could replace each erased qubit with the maximally-mixed state, measure all the stabilizers, and search for any recovery operator supported on the erased qubits, all in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-1473: It is more interesting to study the erasure channel in the cases where the number of errors approaches the code's threshold and not the distance of the code.

elements.sent-2411-1475: Applications of the Graph Formalism We proceed to provide constructive evidence in three areas for the utility of a graph representation.
elements.sent-2411-1476: First, in Section~\ref{subsec:smallcodes , we argue that for near-term experimental purposes, the graph formalism provides a simple and flexible prescription for the design of codes with a desired rate $R$ (where $R = k/n$ and $k$ is the number of logical qubits) and distance $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-1477: As an example of this flexibility, if one wanted to create a code with a particular distance, one can set the degree of the graph in accordance with Proposition~\ref{proposition:degree .}
elements.sent-2411-1478: The degree choice gives an upper bound on the distance, so having a sufficiently large degree is a necessary but not sufficient condition to achieve a code with the desired distance.
elements.sent-2411-1479: To actually get a good distance, one can either check explicitly for small cases, or use graphs which have a provable distance lower bound.

elements.figure-2411-035: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{IcosahedronImproved.jpg} \caption{Double-covering the icosahedron. We begin with an icosahedron, shown in (a). The graph version of this solid is given in (b). To double-cover it, we make two copies of the icosahedron and ``spear" it through the center, illustrated with a solid blue line. elements.sent-2411-1519: In analyzing strategies for decoding, we will find it helpful to introduce a metric, which we call the illumination.
elements.sent-2411-1520: Informally, the illumination of a node $v$ is a weighted count of how many lights are on, out of all those that are toggled by flipping $v$'s switch.
elements.sent-2411-1521: The set of lights toggleable by $v$ are either in $o(v)$ or $oip(v)$ (we ignore the lights which are in both, as flipping $v$'s switch toggles them twice, effectively doing nothing).
elements.sent-2411-1522: We count each light in $o(v)$ as 1 light, but we count each light in $oip(v)$ only as a fraction of a light.
elements.sent-2411-1523: This is because flipping a pivot switch $p \in p(v)$ can toggle many lights in $oip(v)$ at once.
elements.sent-2411-1524: Therefore, we will see that the illumination quantifies how many moves we can save by flipping the switch at $v$.
elements.figure-2411-036: \includegraphics[width=0.28\linewidth]{Illumination.jpg} \caption{A depiction of the illumination of a node $v$ for a given configuration of lights. The set $L=o(v)\,\D\,oip(v)$ does not include the node directly below $v$, as it is connected by both a pink edge (denoting the paths from $v$ to $o(v)$) and a green edge (denoting the paths from $v$ to $oip(v)$). As there are 3 lights on in $o(v)\cap L$, this contributes 3 to the illumination of $v$. One of two lights is on in $oip(v)\cap L$, which contributes $\frac12$ to the illumination. The max illumination is 5, which can be computed by turning on all the lights in the figure, or by adding $|o(v)\cap L|=4$ and all pivot neighbours $p$ of $v$ such that $oi(p)$ has at least one light in $L$.} \label{fig:illumination}
elements.sent-2411-1525: Let $G = (V, E, \set{o, i, p )$ be a graph with input, output, and valid pivot nodes.}

elements.sent-2411-1534: Additionally, note that while the illumination $\L$ depends on a contextual ``current'' configuration of lights, the max-illumination $\L_\text{max}$ depends only on the graph and its assignment of inputs and pivots.
elements.sent-2411-1535: It is useful to compare the notion of illumination to a classical analog.
elements.sent-2411-1536: Classically, when considering a Tanner graph of a code, we may label each data node by the number of check nodes it neighbours which are violated.
elements.sent-2411-1537: Decoders such as the flip algorithm from \citet{sipser1996expander use this metric to correctly deduce the error, by flipping data bits whose node has more violated syndromes than not.}
elements.sent-2411-1538: The notion of illumination is quite similar in that it also captures violated syndromes around a node.
elements.sent-2411-1539: In our graph representation however, the presence of pivots complicates how we measure the amount of violation, giving rise to the more technical definition of illumination.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Greedy graph decoder} \label{alg:greedy} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{FMain}{GreedyDecoder} \SetKwProg{Fn}{Function}{:}{} \Fn{\FMain{$G$}}{ $\text{explored} \leftarrow [],\, (v_0, n_0) \leftarrow (\text{nil}, 0)$\; \While(// STAGE 1: X error recovery loop){True}{ \For{$v \in \CO \cup \CP$}{ $\text{gap} \leftarrow 2\L(v)-\L_\text{max}(v)$\; \tcp{If the illumination of $v$ would decrease by applying $X(v)$, `gap' is positive} \If{$\text{gap} > n_0$}{ $v_0 = v,\, n_0 = \text{gap}$\; } } \If{$v_0 \in explored$ or $n_0 < 1$}{ break\; } Recover$_X(v_0)$ \tcp{toggle lights at $o(v_0) \,\D\, oip(v_0)$, destroy light at $v_0$} Append $v_0$ to explored\; } $\text{explored} \leftarrow [],\, (v_0, n_0) \leftarrow (\text{nil}, 0)$ \tcp{Reset tracking variables} \While(// STAGE 2: Z error on pivots recovery loop){True}{ \For{$v \in \CP$}{ $\text{gap} \leftarrow 2\L(i(v))-\L_\text{max}(i(v))$\; \If{$\text{gap} > n_0$}{ $v_0 = v,\, n_0 = \text{gap}$\; } } \If{$v_0 \in explored$ or $n_0<1$}{ break\; } Recover$_Z(v_0)$ \tcp{toggle lights at $oi(v_0)$} Append $v_0$ to explored\; } \For(// STAGE 3: Z error on non-pivot outputs recovery loop){$v \in \CO$}{ \If{LightIsOn$(v)$}{ Recover$_Z(v)$ \tcp{Destroy light at $v$} } } \KwRet }
elements.figure-2411-037: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{GreedyDecoder.jpg} \caption{Visualization of Algorithm~\ref{alg:greedy} run on the final graph in Fig.~\ref{fig:QLO_Decoding}. Inputs are blue triangles, pivots are red diamonds, and non-pivot outputs are black circles. In (a)-(c), non-pivot output nodes are labeled with their illumination gaps, given by $2 \L(v) - \L_{\max}(v)$, where $\L$ and $\L_{\max}$ are given in Defintion~\ref{def:illumination}. These are the nodes checked by the $X$ loop. In (d)-(e), the illumination gaps of inputs are labeled; these are the nodes checked by the $Z$-pivot loop. The final recovery map shown is precisely the error we applied originally to the graph in Fig.~\ref{fig:QLO_Decoding}.} \label{fig:GreedyDecoder}
elements.sent-2411-1540: The greedy decoder, Algorithm~\ref{alg:greedy , is a strategy in the decoding instance of QLO.}
elements.sent-2411-1541: In particular, the operations permitted are Recover$_X$ and Recover$_Z$, which apply the corresponding Paulis that effectively flip switches or destroy lights depending on the node $v$ chosen.
elements.sent-2411-1542: However, applying $X$ to $v$ toggles both $o(v)$ and $oip(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-1543: Toggling $oip(v)$ is the same as toggling $oi(p)$ for each pivot $p$ in $p(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-1544: Since this can be toggled by applying a single $Z$ to $p$ (effectively toggling ``the light at $p$'', or equivalently, $oi(p)$, we treat the status of the lights in $oi(p)$ as having the same collective importance as one light in $o(v)$, and we therefore normalize $oi(p)$'s contribution to the gap by dividing the respective term by $|oi(p)|$ for each $p$.
elements.sent-2411-1545: This is all summed up by the aggregate quantity $\L(v)$, the illumination of $v$ as defined in Definition~\ref{def:illumination .}
elements.sent-2411-1546: At each step, we apply the single move that will most decrease the illumination of any given node, starting with nodes in $\CO\cup\CP$ and continuing with nodes in $\CI$.
elements.sent-2411-1547: We only perform moves that decrease the illumination by at least 1, as that is how many moves it would take to decrease it by other means.

elements.sent-2411-1550: When the algorithm terminates, we observe that the recovery operation is precisely the original Pauli error applied, indicating that the greedy algorithm decoded correctly in this example.
elements.sent-2411-1551: Applying Recover$_X$ may itself turn on more lights in other places, so to avoid issues related to parallelism, we work in sequence and apply $X$ recoveries one at a time, in order of what most decreases the node's illumination, a process we will refer to as the first stage of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1552: They are detected by stabilizers in $oi(v)$, so we apply the analogous greedy recovery operation there in the second stage.
elements.sent-2411-1553: Finally, for remaining lights on in $v \in \CO$, we directly destroy the lights with $Z$ operations in the third and final stage of Algorithm~\ref{alg:greedy .}

elements.sent-2411-1555: In this definition, we are specifically interested in how much can an operation at node $v$ change the illumination of another node.
elements.sent-2411-1556: Let $G = (V, E, \set{o, i, p )$ be a graph with with vertices $V = \CI \sqcup \CP \sqcup \CO$, inputs $\CI$, pivots $\CP$, non-pivot outputs $\CO$, edges $E$, and $o, i, p$ as in Definition~\ref{def:neighbour_sets .}}
elements.sent-2411-1557: We say that $G$ is $B$-sensitive or that the sensitivity of $G$ is $B$, where $B\in\mathbb{R}$ is the maximum amount by which the illumination $\L(v)$ of any node can change from the application of certain single-qubit Pauli operations, when starting with all lights off.
elements.sent-2411-1558: Specifically, if $v\in\CO\cup\CP$, we allow any Pauli operation and if $v\in\CI$, we only consider Pauli $Z$ operations.
elements.sent-2411-1559: Quantitatively, denoting the change in the illumination of $v$ due to applying the Pauli $P$ on vertex $w$ by $\D_{P(w) (\L(v))$, we have \begin{equation}B\coloneqq\max\left(\max_{\substack{v,w\in\CO\cup\CP w\neq v P\in\set{X,Y,Z}}}\D_{P(w)}(\L(v)),\max_{\substack{v\in\CI w\in\CO\cup\CP w\neq p(v)}}\D_{Z(w)}(\L(v))\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1560: To give some intuition about what the notion of sensitivity represents, imagine a graph representing a social network in which nodes are people and edges connect people who are friends.
elements.sent-2411-1561: If it is common for two vertices (people) to have many shared neighbours (mutual friends), then it will be possible to toggle many lights on around the first vertex by flipping the switch on the second.
elements.sent-2411-1562: If mutual friends are scarce and two nodes never share more than one mutual friend, then it will be difficult to turn on many lights around a given vertex by flipping switches at other vertices.
elements.sent-2411-1563: The precise description of illumination used in calculating sensitivity also includes terms such as $oip(v)$, which care about more distant vertices than merely the immediate neighbours, but counting mutual friends can serve as a good heuristic for the sensitivity of a graph before inputs and pivots have been assigned.
elements.sent-2411-1564: Altogether, the sensitivity definition forms a sufficient condition to place a relatively tight guarantee on the distance of the code and the greedy decoder's performance.
elements.sent-2411-1565: Let $G = (V, E, \set{o, i, p )$ be a graph with vertices $V = \CI \sqcup \CP \sqcup \CO$, inputs $\CI$, pivots $\CP$, non-pivot outputs $\CO$, edges $E$, and $o, i, p$ as in Definition~\ref{def:neighbour_sets .}}
elements.sent-2411-1566: We define the brightness $\ell\in\mathbb{N}$ of graph $G$ as the minimum max-illumination across all vertices in $V$, as defined in Definition~\ref{def:illumination .}
elements.sent-2411-1567: Specifically, \begin{equation}\label{eq:delta_*} \ell\coloneqq \min_{v\in V}\L_max(v),\end{equation} where $\L_{\max}$ is given in Definition~\ref{def:illumination .}
elements.sent-2411-1568: Note that by definition, $\ell \leq \min_{v \in V \deg(v)$ since $\L_{\max (v) \leq \deg(v)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1569: By Proposition~\ref{proposition:degree , $\ell \leq \operatorname{dist (C(G))$, where $C(G)$ is the code corresponding to $G$ and $\operatorname{dist}$ is the distance.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Simple input-pivot assigner} \label{alg:simple_input} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{FMain}{SimpleInputPivot} \SetKwProg{Fn}{Function}{:}{} \Fn{\FMain{$G$}}{ \For{$v \in V$}{ $\text{marked}_{v} \leftarrow \text{False}$\; } \While{$\exists v, w \in V \,:\, (v, w) \in E \text{ and } \text{marked}_{v} = \text{marked}_{w} = \text{False}$}{ $\text{marked}_{v} \leftarrow \text{True}$\; $\text{marked}_{w} \leftarrow \text{True}$\; $\text{set } v \text{ as input}$\; $\text{set } w \text{ as pivot}(v)$\; \For{$u \in N(N(v) \cup N(w))$}{ $\text{marked}_{u} \leftarrow \text{True}$\; } } \KwRet }
elements.figure-2411-040: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{AssignmentAlgoSimple.jpg} \caption{Example run of the simple input-pivot assignment process given by Algorithm~\ref{alg:simple_input}. Initially, all nodes are free, depicted by black circles. We begin in (a) by picking a connected free pair (orange squares). One is assigned as input (blue triangle) and the other as pivot (red diamond). Both the neighbours of the pair and the neighbours of those neighbours are grayed out in (b), making them no longer free. We repeat this process in (c)--(e) until there are no free pairs. The algorithm then terminates with the final assignment as in (f).} \label{fig:AssignmentAlgoSimple}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Clever input-pivot assigner} \label{alg:input} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{FMain}{CleverInputPivot} \SetKwProg{Fn}{Function}{:}{} \Fn{\FMain{$G, \g$}}{ $\CD_{\text{min}} \leftarrow \min_{v \in V} \deg(v)$\; \For{$v \in V$}{ $\text{availability}_{v} \leftarrow \lfloor \CD_{\text{min}}/\g \rfloor$\; $\text{marked}_{v} \leftarrow \text{False}$\; } \While{$\exists v_1, v_2 \in V \,:\, (v_1, v_2) \in E \text{ and } \text{marked}_{v_1} = \text{marked}_{v_2} = \text{False}$}{ $\text{set } v_1 \text{ as input}$\; $\text{set } v_2 \text{ as pivot}(v_1)$\; $\text{marked}_{v_1} \leftarrow \text{True},\, \text{marked}_{v_2} \leftarrow \text{True}$\; \For{$w \in (N(v_1) \cup N(v_2)) \setminus \set{v_1, v_2}$}{ $\text{marked}_w \leftarrow \text{True}$\; $\text{availability}_{w} \leftarrow \text{availability}_{w} - 1$\; \If{$w \in N(v_1) \cap N(v_2) \text{ and } \text{availability}_{w} > 0$}{ $\text{availability}_{w} \leftarrow \text{availability}_{w} - 1$\; \tcp{Availability decrements once for each time it appears in a neighbouring set, unless already 0} } \If{$\text{availability}_{w} = 0$}{ \For{$u \in N(w)$}{ $\text{marked}_u \leftarrow \text{True}$\; } } } } \KwRet }
elements.figure-2411-041: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{AssignmentAlgoClever.jpg} \caption{Example run of the clever input-pivot assignment process given by Algorithm~\ref{alg:input}. The graph shown has minimum degree $\CD_{\min} = 4$ and we choose the parameter $\gamma = 2$, so that the availability is $\CD_{\min} / \gamma = 4/2 = 2$. The graph begins in (a) with all nodes free (shown as black circles) with availability 2 on every node. We choose some connected free pair (orange squares) and assign one as input (blue triangle) and the other as pivot (red diamond). The neighbours are ``grayed out", i.e. marked as not free, and their availability is decreased in (b). The availability of each neighbour drops by 1 if it neighbours only the pivot or only the input, or 2 if it neighbours both the input and the pivot. In (c), all nodes whose availabilities drop to 0 have their own neighbours grayed out. This process is repeated in (d) and (e), and so on, until the final assignment is reached in (f).} \label{fig:AssignmentAlgoClever}

elements.sent-2411-1898: Key code properties (weight, distance, encoding and logical operator circuit depths) are all bounded by small linear functions of graph degrees, and key coding algorithms (distance approximation, minimum weight generator selection, decoding) are all strategies on various instances of quantum lights out (QLO) games.
elements.sent-2411-1899: A second pressing direction involves more deeply studying the quantum lights out game, as insights onto their strategies have equivalent interpretations on coding algorithm performance.
elements.sent-2411-1900: This paper studied the simplest QLO strategy and derived performance guarantees for graphs of sufficient girth.
elements.sent-2411-1901: Such a connection is reminiscent of belief propagation insights from classical coding theory, which led to significant progress for classical error correction.
elements.sent-2411-1902: By devising more clever QLO strategies, could we weaken the constraints on graphs decoded by the strategy, and therefore construct efficiently QLO-decodable codes with distance large enough to be practical? A third direction is directly constructive.
elements.sent-2411-1903: Given particular architectures or algorithms of experimental interest, is it possible to systematically design graphs whose codes are particularly suitable for such devices or algorithms in terms of locality of connectivity, geometry, rate, and distance? As a starting point, one can conduct a more thorough investigation into extremal graph theory to obtain the best explicit constructions of graphs with a particular girth (as dictated by the Master Theorem of girth, Theorem~\ref{thm:high_girth_high_dist ).}
elements.sent-2411-1904: Such graphs come with guaranteed efficient decodability and distance lower bounds nearly matching the upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-1905: Even further, one can devise nontrivial strategies for selecting a better choice of stabilizer generators under certain structural conditions, in hopes of chaining these ideas together in order to create high distance, low check weight, and efficiently decodable stabilizer codes.
elements.sent-2411-1906: It is plausible that such approaches involve modifying the representation itself.
elements.sent-2411-1907: If there exists a collection of graphs that, in exchange for representing a restricted subset of stabilizer codes, satisfies low-weight stabilizers, it may open new paths forward for practical code constructions and corresponding algorithms that are fundamentally not based on topological or classical (i.e. CSS-type product) reasoning.
